The kingdom rallied, its soldiers bolstered, and they fought with everything they had. The branches of the great golden tree seemed to protect the besieged capital as they hung above it. The next two years were hard fought, but with a rejuvenated people, born in the fires of desperate struggle, they persisted. And then one morning they awoke to a sight of a second golden tree far in the distance, its size almost unfathomable - but with a stark likeness to the tree that grew in their own land. 


Immediately the blight stopped - the blighted lands replaced with foreign climates and terrain, and most importantly people. People who seemed wary of them, who repelled them at their borders. No one was sure what happened - but something clearly had changed. But the blight had stopped, and the void creatures lessened their assault, now being almost exclusively located along their southern borders. It also became clear that their new neighbors were not unified, with some bearing a standard of a golden sun, and others bearing a standard of a gear. Attempts to approach these borders were turned away with violence, and the people of Aura needed a respite from endless war. 


Communication from these border forces was limited, but the message was simple: Do not approach, do not break the blockade, and if they followed these rules it would open the floor for a productive conversation - but not today. The people of Auros quickly understood that those bearing the standard of the sun - supposedly The Empire of Light - had a look in their eyes like they were angry they even had to say that, and they were much more willing to kill anyone who even vaguely approached the blockade. If that wasn't clear enough, the borders with the Empire were strung with the corpses of the people of Auros who had tested their warnings. The border with the standard of the gear - The Unified Technarchy - was more peaceful. They would not allow their warnings to be ignored, but actual death was rare. 


Four months have passed in this stasis, and while this has meant Auros’s future is uncertain, it has also given them a rest that they sorely needed. Now the kingdom stands, and true to their word a Summit has been announced, to be held on Aura’s side of the border. A summit to discuss terms with their neighbors, under the premise of determining Auros’s sovereignty and fate. Astute observers have noticed that troops at each border have only grown, and their forces have slowly transitioned into invasion forces, not just defensive formations. 


And now the kingdom stands, with new neighbors, an uncertain future, and on the eve of a summit that will determine much. The Saint, the de facto leader of Aura, has reassured her people. The Mother will guide her people to what is to come next. Rest, hold your families close, and prepare for the dawn. 
Social/Political Structure
Auros is unique in its political structure. Pre-fall, governance of Aura was centrally controlled with the founding families inter-marrying and creating a royal family in an unbroken line, and with power being shared with the Church of Seasons. When the Calamity came, this political structure quickly broke under the pressure, with pressures on the royal family culminating in them absconding from the throne as voices raised against them during the Season Storm. After that communities largely held themselves together through collective pacts of survival, and each section of Aura that was still standing had its own local leader barely keeping things together. 


After the Eater of Worlds Descended, and the void creatures appeared, all eyes quickly turned to The Saint, who while young, radiated a power that gave the people hope. She, and her brother - who stands as her silent right arm - quickly became the de facto leader. Not by choice, but by the necessity of survival. Largely though, questions of governance have been far from the minds of everyone. Instead, people have formed into communities that hold each other up as much as possible, and make ends meet despite the desperate circumstances. Above all, the words of The Saint are taken as decree, and what she asks for is informally executed by members of The Militia, who have self organized to try and hold back the void. The Saint, for her part, has not completely shied away from this authority, and tried her best to simultaneously lead the desperate battle against the void, and also make just decisions for the general population. 


Since the stabilization against the void has occurred, public calls have been made for The Saint to make a residence of the old Aura throne, but these calls have remained unanswered, and the Aura throne has remained vacant. The topic of how the people of Auros should govern going forward has increasingly been on people’s minds, but in the meantime The Saint has led the people where needed - and intends to represent them at the Summit. It has been whispered, however, that she is searching for other representatives across Auros to help her with the Summit.


Socially there exists tension between those who are called City-Born - families that existed in the Aura capital for at least a few generations, and Wild-Born, who existed in the wilds, and either settled just prior to the Calamity, or shortly afterwards. However, in the face of annihilation both groups came together to survive the end, and only recently the friction between these two groups has come back to the surface. Friction that is felt more directly, however, is between those who served on the front line against the battle against the void, and those who didn't. Those who did leave home to fight the void, or who supported the front in some clear way, are being held in high esteem, and as informal governance groups start to form in the absence of clear leadership, those positions are almost always people who served on the front. Those people, having fought an almost hopeless war for eight years, have come back home with more than their fair share of emotional scars, and questions of if they should be the ones to govern are just barely kept below the surface. Those who did not serve on the front directly are beginning to form into a less respected caste of people - but the lines between who did or didn’t serve on the front, and what exactly constitutes supporting the front is a grey area that has caused a lot of tension. Moreover, many families sent all those who are able to fight to the front, and those people never returned - but with no formal records of this, accusations of support or non-support are high in the air. These tensions are in their very early days of formation, however, and it’s not clear in which direction they will move.


Socially, Auros is defined by its transitional period - no clear leadership has emerged with The Saint seemingly being uninterested in governance, and in that absence a vacuum of power is being actively felt.
Religion
The vast majority of the religious worship within Auros is in the name of The Mother of Rebirth. The Mother of Rebirth does not have a strictly organized church, but The Saint has trained clerics directly, who give sermons and are often seen on the field of battle. Dedicating yourself to The Mother of Rebirth comes with a silent understanding that you dedicate yourself to the people of Auros directly, and clerics are often informally posted to positions near the battlefield. In recent months, this clerical force has been more focused on recovery, and with helping to rebuild as much as possible. Active discussion is being held to discuss formal organization and structure, but with The Saint’s attention stretched thin, this has not culminated into any official proclamation. 


Worship of The Watcher of the Seasons - the unseen, but still worshiped, entity that presumably resided in the tower - still exists. Those who still follow these practices argue that while The Mother of Rebirth did save the people of Auros, the only reason they made it that far was because The Saint was taken in and given the power of the seasons. This, they say, is a continuing gift from The Watcher themselves, who knew hard times were coming. However, finding those still faithful to The Watcher is limited, but they are more organized given their previous structure and authority. They still, however, seem able to call upon power in some limited circumstance, which has further allowed them to stay vaguely relevant. 


A common song heard in Aurosian calls to worship is the following:
Audio
Sheet Music
Technology Level
Feudal-society level technology paired with high magic. Technology in and of itself is very limited, but magic is abundant. Culturally almost everyone who is capable of wielding a weapon is trained to use it. Much of the actual architecture bears the signs of battle and war, with rebuilding efforts still in their infancy. Towering over the last city of Auros is a golden glowing Tree, and this tree features prominently in artistic and architectural decisions that are being made in the rebuilding effort. The standard of Auros is a glowing golden tree on a black background. 
Aesthetic
The Aurosian Aesthetic still resembles their seasonal upbringing. Whites with Icy blues, Reds, Oranges, and Greens are all common with a warm vibrancy in the amount of color they wear. Clothing is often monotone or aesthetically fit to a single season, or deliberately constructed to represent seasons fading into one another. (a dress with a fiery skirt, giving way to softer oranges, and then eventually to a white shawl and icy makeup, for example). As time has gone on, golden accents and colored clothing have grown more and more popular, as has iconography representing their golden tree. 
The Lost
Auros spent nine years fighting against The Void, and as a result The Lost are a much more common phenomenon within the Aurosian population. Originally, The Lost were treated with fear and hostility - not being allowed to enter Aura, and being forced to stay in the unsafe outskirts and abandoned villages of the wilds. This changed, however, when a powerful Lost calling themselves Fade recruited their fellow Lost and formed The Shattered Brigade. As the Void crept further and further towards Aura, The Shattered Brigade found themselves as first responders to Void incursions, slowly gaining trust. When a gargantuan Void creature threatened to destroy the gates of Aura it was The Shattered Brigade that fought them off, and The Saint offered for them to rejoin Aura officially. The Shattered Brigade accepted, and settled again in Aura - forming what is called the Lost District. Today many still look at The Lost with distrust and fear, but those who have fought with them on the front line fight for their inclusion in society.
The Shattered Brigade
The Shattered Brigade is loosely organized into five small squads of 8-15 people, each with a Captain, who in turn reports to Fade - broadly considered a Commander with absolute authority. How each squad then organizes themselves in turn is up to each individual Captain. Opinions of Fade as a leader are generally positive, but they have demonstrated the ability to act with brutal efficiency from time to time, and as a result loyalty to Fade is also tinged with fear. This hierarchy is not officially upheld by any written rules or arrangement, but rather through informal agreements. Most Captains are from the early days of the Brigade, and as a result all have a strong loyalty to Fade directly. Formally, the only official members of the Brigade are Lost themselves - the typical operations they perform are to combat the Void directly, and Fade has broadly decided that non-Lost members represent intolerable risk. Despite that, some squads do have non-Lost members-by-proxy who operate in a support capacity. As Auros looks to the future, and the formal military conflict that it might bring, Brigade members are developing conflicting opinions of how the Brigade should operate going forward. 


Within the Shattered Brigade are five squads, each headed by a captain. Each squad has their own area of expertise - outlined loosely below.


      * Night’s Shield, headed by Captain Isla. Night’s Shield specializes in defensive positions and forms the combat backbone of any defense operation the Shattered Brigade operates in. Currently heavily involved keeping the Void at bay near The Grey in Aurosian lands.
      * Blue Harvest, headed by Captain Leishen. Originally a group of farmers who banded together to try to keep the countryside able to produce food and grow, even as the Void crept in. What originally started as a group known for their hit-and-run tactics eventually turned into a full stealth and recon unit.  
      * Second Sunrise, headed by Captain Arawan. Specialized as first responders and civilian evacuators, and broadly operates as a flexible combat unit to deal with emerging problems in the fight against the Void.
      * Ink & Iron, headed by Captain Splice. Specialized crafters and magical researchers helping The Shattered Brigade keep in cutting edge anti-Void arms and armor.
      * The Fist, headed by Captain Brigid (presumed dead). A team focused on deep-Void striking, able to get in, solve a whole lot of problems, and get out. Rumor has it that about a year ago Captain Brigid took a sizable portion of their squad against orders and left for somewhere deep in the Void strewn lands of Auros and never returned.


Crime in Auros
Auros is a fairly peaceful society, and crime of any sort is uncommon. After spending nearly a decade communally fighting for their lives, premeditated violence between citizens is rare, and usually reserved for the occasional crime of passion. However, the years of war against the Void and ongoing blockade have taken their toll, and a small black market (more tailored to food, medicine, and basic supplies than weapons) can sometimes be found.
Common Expressions/Holidays
Expressions are not exhaustive - they are meant to give a vibe and baseline.


      * “The Seasons Tell”: Used to describe that in time, what is true, and what is good will hold out. The future is harsh, but what needs to happen will happen, and who can survive will be seen. Said commonly in response to hard or difficult situations.  
      * “Lay me in her boughs”: Said as a salute to death. Originally formed on the front lines from soldiers as a way of accepting that the upcoming battle may be their last. Has spread to be used more generally in exasperation, embarrassment, or humor.
      * “Cotton Handed”: Sometimes shortened to “Cottoned” or “Cotton” is used as a derogatory term for those who did not serve - or were perceived to not serve - on the direct front line. Increasingly used in arguments about who should and shouldn’t have elevated rights in the society Auros is to become. 
      * Mother’s Day: Celebrated in Fall, signifies the day The Mother descended from the heavens and the Great Tree grew. A great celebration is held at the base of the tree/ruined tower focused on song, drink, and appreciation of life. In the first, and only, Mother’s Day everyone was asked by the Saint to write a note to someone in your life telling them what they do that makes you appreciate living. These letters were given and exchanged at the festival - which is being held as a nascent tradition. In the first year this was held (just two months ago) the celebration, which was supposed to just last a single day, lasted for five. Filled with laughter, but also tears, Mother’s Day represented a watershed moment for the collective tensions of the people of Auros. On the final day of celebrations the people were so inspired that a no-named singer appeared from the throng of the Auros people and delivered a song that echoed across the celebration. All were transfixed as they listened to the beauty and sorrow the song spoke of, and all were deeply moved. Ten minutes later the person disappeared into the crowd and the festivities began to wrap up. It is said that the song was a gift from The Mother of Rebirth herself, and its notes still ache in everyone’s hearts.
Naming Conventions


Aurosian given names often seem antiquated to other civilizations. Last names were a recent invention after the building of the great city around the tower and the need for differentiation grew as people gathered in the Kingdom of Aura. Families are often named after either the roles they perform for people in preparing for new seasons or their function in the city, with a portion of clans making names fashioned after their most prominent powerful or famous members.


Example first names:
Vivaine, Linnet, Taran, Evander, Callan, Wesley, Wynn, Rhys, Deykin, Howell, Finan, Cormac, Ciara, Gillie, Aillenn


Example family names:
Smith, Tiller, Weaver, Sower, Lannerett, Offendal




________________


The Astari
The Astari


Synopsis
The Astari people inhabit an incredibly vibrant and lush swath of land taking up roughly a third of the habitable land around the Wellspring. Incredibly insular, and not tolerant against uninvited approach to their territory, the Astari fiercely defend their forests. What is more, the forest itself seems to fiercely defend the Astari as well - gifts from their deity The Oak Father. This symbiotic relationship has earned their lands a second name - The Rampant Green. Despite their insular nature, the Astari are known as good neighbors - so long as no one attempts to cross the threshold into their own lands. 


Meeting an Astari is incredibly rare, for few venture outside, and when they do it is for a specific trading purpose - or to help defend the lands of the various small groups of people who have made shelter close to, but not within, the Astari lands.


[Be sure to read the , and the The Main Camera of the Story section when considering playing a character from The Astari. Reach out to staff if you need help developing a character.]
Lineage Population
Common: Aewen, Chimera, Human, Oaksworn
Rare: Forged, Lost, Ogrim 
Very Rare: Underkin
History
When the universe was young The Oak Father - creator of all life in the universe - was going about his work placing the stars in the sky. He placed these stars so that the people who would live could see what they were doing. This task was mighty, and so he created Star Rays - colossal creatures that could travel the void of the universe - to help him. These Star Rays would pick up the stars from The Oak Father’s basket, and together carry them into the sky so they could light the universe. 


One day, when the families of Star Rays were taking a star to the far end of the universe, one of the Star Rays - Ash’Haie - became separated from the pack and lost. Scared and alone, Ash’Haie travelled the distant universe looking for its kin - but they were never found. 


The Oak Father, now with parts of the universe lit by the stars, began creating living things to inhabit the various planets. One of those things were the Astari people, who went about living and growing. One night when the Astari were welcoming the turning of the cycle in song a curious creature found them - Ash’Haie. Ash’Haie had been lost for so long, and when it heard the Astari people it sought them out, hoping to find friends for its loneliness. The Astari people saw the colossal Star-Ray settle in the dirt near their village, and they could all sense its sadness. Not wanting Ash’Haie to live alone and in sorrow, the Astari climbed upon its back, which was covered with rock and trees, and massive mountains resembling shells of the sea. They spoke to Ash’Haie, and vowed to protect it and keep it company, and Ash’Haie felt its hope return. 


With that agreement, and with the Astari safely on its back, Ash’Haie took off again for the stars, searching still for its lost kin. The Astari did their part, settling in the massive hollow shell mountains upon its back, keeping the lands and creatures around them safe. Occasionally creatures from the great void of space would land upon Ash’Haie to feed from its flesh, and the Astari would repel them and make sure no damage was done. 


Then, one afternoon the Astari people felt Ash’Haie’s mood shift in anxiety - for Ash’Haie had felt a distant call it could not ignore. Ash’Haie rushed through space much faster than before, and the Astari people could feel that it strained itself greatly. Trees upon its back began to lose their verdancy, and creatures of the void became more common. While the Astari became anxious if they could hold out, they knew in their hearts that Ash’Haie was doing what needed to be done, and they trusted that all would be well. A short time after, the Astari saw a massive golden tree appear in the distance, and on one section of the land around this tree sat a sprawling forest. Ash’Haie descended upon the forest, landing in its soil, and burrowing into the land.


The Astari could sense that as this happened, Ash’Haie’s anxiety fell, and that they were safe, although confused. As the Astari settled into their new home and explored the strange new forest they also began suspecting that they were not alone - something in the forest looked upon them with curiosity. Time passed, and the story of the Astari people’s travels and history was told to ease anxieties. Then, one hot summer night the Astari’s greatest Flesh-Singers were called upon to enter the forest from a distant voice. 


The Flesh-Singers answered, and within it they were given the most marvelous of sights - for within their new forest was The Oak Father himself, creator of all living things the stars light touch. The Oak Father told them that the great task set upon by the Star Rays was finally complete, and no other stars were needed to be placed. He told them that he had created this land so that the other Star Rays could settle here now that their job was done, and that he was overjoyed to see that the long lost Ash’Haie had heard the call first. The other Star Rays were far from here, having just lit the farthest of the universe's reaches, but that they would some day come. He was originally surprised to see that Ash’Haie had such small friends, but after observing them from afar he knew the goodness in their hearts. 


The Oak Father, however, had a word of warning to the Astari people - others had heard the call to the Star Rays as well, and they would not be so kind to the gifts The Oak Father had prepared. This land must be protected so that the Star Rays would have a place to rest their tired bodies. He asked The Astari if they would aid him in defending these lands, and the Astari agreed.


The Oak Father’s words of warning were not without truth - for shortly after people of all sorts appeared at the forest's edge. First smaller groups who were easily turned away, but then by heavily armored warriors bearing symbols of horses and gears. The Astari, however, were prepared - and with the power of The Oak Father at their backs they repelled these invaders. 


Other groups began settling nearby - smaller civilizations who had heard the call but did not wish to fight, and they were allowed to shelter in the very edges of the great forest. With these truces settled, the warriors of gear and horses began holding off their assaults, and while they do occasionally try to sneak in, active war has ceased. The Astari people, however, are resolute in their charge, and remain ready. 


As time has gone on The Oak Father has called upon some Astari to help him directly, sometimes taking them within private groves meant only for The Oak Father. He gives some of those attendants tasks outside of the great forest itself - often to retrieve lost relics of his own power. Life has gone well, and while adjusting to the task of defense has been difficult, the Astari people find abundance in their lands.


Three years ago, one of The Astari’s neighbors - the people of Faulbrook - were being overcome by creatures of the void, which had overwhelmed their lands. The Oak Father had been fond of Faulbrook’s fallen goddess, and took pity on the Faulbrook people who were facing oblivion. The Oak Father asked the Astari to provide refuge to their people and allow them into their lands. The Astari mobilized, providing cover for their escape, and letting the ravaged populations settle near the main Astari village. 


While tensions have existed between these two people, the people of Faulbrook, calling themselves Brook Borne, have largely adapted to a life in communion, rather than domain, over nature. The Brook Borne, in turn, have assisted heavily in any traveling excursions the Astari need to take - themselves being much more accustomed to life outside the great forest. One particular Brook Borne, Antur, a promising runewright, has pledged herself completely to The Oak Father, and has been aiding the Oak Father in his works.


Recently, the Astari have heard of a curious new arrival to The Wellspring, and this land - Auros - has a tree bearing the signs of the Wellspring, which in and of itself is a gift from The Oak Father. The Oak Father has asked that an envoy be sent to their lands to greet them and learn the truth of their arrival.
Society/Culture
The Astari people exist spread across three shell-homes, small mountains that rise from the forest on the back of Ash’Haie. Homes and villages are built against and within the mountains themselves, and form into communal villages. Each of the three villages are operated communally, with individuals performing duties for their communities that are assigned by a council of elders at a young age. Whenever an Astari crosses their twenty-fifth birthday they undergo a process called The Choosing, where they are summoned by elders of their communities who will, in conjunction with the Astari’s wishes and aptitudes, decide on what their role in society will be. Disputes between individuals or groups are expected to be resolved between each other directly, and when this cannot be done the process is overseen by an elder, who tries to reach a conclusion that equally satisfies and dissatisfies each party. Being unable to come to an agreement without oversight, however, is seen as a social failing, and Astari try to avoid this wherever possible. 


Society itself is very influenced by its druidic roots, and as a result is focused on living in communion with the natural world. Protection and maintaining a balance with nature is considered a core tenant of Astari society as a whole, and natural wardens are a role chosen during The Choosing. While private places for individual families do exist, the vast majority of space in Astari villages is communal, and life in close proximity to others is considered normal.


Astari Oaksworn are slow to mature, reaching an age of adulthood at 30 with some living well into their second century. Astari Oaksworn babies gestate for a period of on average 13 months.


In recent years, decisions of The Astari society, as a whole, have been guided in part by The Oak Father, who imparts his wisdom to the elders.
Religion
Druidism is the dominant religion by far, but takes many forms. Some Astari consider themselves Flesh-Singers, who attempt to commune with Ash’Haie directly, and take the protection of its health very seriously. For Flesh-Singers stories, traditions, and ceremonies are often done through either singing or oral storytelling. Since settling around the Wellspring, worship of The Oak Father directly has become incredibly common, especially amongst those who defend the lands of the great forest directly. This is not in conflict with any religious practice, as The Oak Father has long been a traditional mythological feature within the Astari history, and his tenants are in unison with Astari society.
Technology Level
Astari technology is incredibly basic, but is augmented and aided by powerful druidic magic. Flora and Fauna seem to respond to the call of Astari directly, and aid in Astari villages. The most dedicated and connected Astari druids and Flesh-Singers can command the defense of large swaths of the great forest, which make a fierce defense network. 
Aesthetic
Astari aesthetic and clothing resembles their relationship with their environment. Clothing often has clearly identifiable plant material, or things woven/crafted to resemble animals or living things. Oftentimes Astari will wear woven ponchos that make them resemble Ash’Haie when their arms are out. Equipment, furnishings, and construction follow these motifs - often incorporating living trees into the construction of their homes. Brook Borne tends to favor simple wear that doubles as good work-wear. Muted colors, easily cleaned are the primary fashion style of those who survived. 
Crime Among the Astari
The Astari, due to their insularity, are perhaps the least involved in smuggling or information brokering of the Wellspring civilizations, which causes information and goods (especially poisons and medicines) from the Astari provided on the black market to be particularly valuable. The forests are well defended from outsiders by the Astari - and some say by the Oak Father himself - and their communal culture means internal crime is uncommon. However, tensions do occasionally flare between the Astari and the community of Faulbrook, which can lead to infrequent arguments, assaults, or theft.
Common Expressions/Holidays
Expressions are not exhaustive - they are meant to give a vibe and baseline.

      * “The Woods are Deep”: Used to recognize that some truths will never be known, and an answer cannot be provided for everything. The woods are deep - many things will lie hidden never to be discovered, and that is okay.
      * “Plough Headed”: Recently invented slang to tease Brook Borne that have tendencies towards fighting against nature, instead of in harmony with it. Broadly used to also describe people who cannot find contentment and are grasping for more. 
      * “Honey Eyed”: Often shortened to “honeyed” - this phrase comes from the Brook Borne and refers to people who are too idealistic, or not willing to do hard work to achieve their goals. Refers to people who are too focused on the benefits of collective labor instead of the labor itself - specifically benefits like drinking mead, a Faulbrook specialty. Calling someone Honey Eyed is often said in response to people being called Plough Headed. 
      * Harvest Festival: Brook Borne festival celebrating the time of the year when their harvest came due. Celebrates both the hard work of communities and their people, their fallen goddess Clea - the goddess of the harvest - and to give thanks to the bountiful harvest. Traditionally celebrated using a lot of light browns and crafts made of dried hay and browning trees. Since moving into Astari lands, these decorations have had to be sourced from further abroad. The Astari originally disapproved of this holiday, but as time has gone on they have used it as an opportunity to support their new neighbors and understand their way of life, as well as the tragedy they all experienced. 
      * Day of Promise: Astari festival celebrating when the ancestral Astari climbed upon Ash’Haie’s back and made their vows of protection. During the festival, Astari make ceremonies around vows they are making to a cause, a person, or a community. Often bonding announcements are made during the Day of Promise - but so are dedications of platonic commitment.
Naming Conventions


Astari often have segmented names that grow the longer an Astari lives. The first segment in their names are distinct to each Astari, and when an Astari dies the name is free to be given to a new child. Most Astari go by their first segment colloquially and will only give their full name in formal settings and introductions. Names are a legacy to live up to and tales of your namesake's past deeds are passed on through oral tradition. Astari have no last names, if pressed they will say they are <First Name> of Ash'Haie.


Example Names:
Sel’Thas’Oril, Orn’Ho’Modan, Adi’Fa’Maen
________________


The Empire of Light
The Empire of Light
Synopsis
The Empire of Light stands as the greatest military and cultural force that inhabits the Wellspring. Befitting its strength, it also occupies the greatest swath of land, and maintains a strong military presence across its borders. Conquest is never far from its mind, as The Steed - the one rightful god - demands this of its people, and not a year goes by without its neighbors preparing for potential incursion. The people it conquers, it absorbs into its Radiant Light - offering those who find the glory of The Steed a better, stronger, future. Those who cannot find its light, or maintain worship of lesser gods, are put to the sword - as The Steed demands. 


Four years ago The Empire of Light launched its greatest crusade - The Crimson Dawn - rapidly expanding its territory, and even reaching as far as setting up a citadel at the foot of the Wellspring, land that up to that point had been occupied by The United Technarchy. However, this victory was short lived, as treacherous enemies sabotaged the Citadel from within, allowing the Empire’s enemies to breach the Citadel’s defenses. What’s worse, the duplicitous cowards awoke the Demons of the Wellspring, who ruined the Citadel and crushed the routed Legion, and many of the forces pursuing them. Now, the Citadel and its immediate surroundings are a blasted land, the Demons of the Wellspring allowing no one to come near. 


[Be sure to read the , and the The Main Camera of the Story section when considering playing a character from The Empire of Light. Reach out to staff if you need help developing a character.]
Lineage Population
Common: Aewen, Chimera, Human, Ogrim, Underkin
Rare: Forged, Lost, Oaksworn 
History
The Empire of Light has proudly stood tall as a civilization for over four hundred years. In its distant history it existed as a world filled with warring nations. Then, as the legends tell, The Steed descended from the heavens and declared the site of a great recent battle to be a holy land, and the people who could occupy it for long enough (and thereby prove to The Steed that they were the most deserving) would be granted the stars in The Steed’s Radiance. So a great and bloody war was fought that spanned 100 years. The victors held the land for a decade, and when the last of their enemies fell on their ramparts, The Steed descended, and from their enemies’ broken bones and bloodstained armor, The Steed gave the first of his gifts, the Blood Angels. 


The Blood Angels, with wings of pure white and forms of flowing blood, built weapons stronger than any have seen and cities of paradise for the victorious warriors. The rest of the world either fell to their knees in tribute, or fell on the swords of the empowered champions - and thus was born The Empire of Light. The names of defeated nations have been lost to history, for only the Empire of Light shall stand the test of time - a nation of The Steed’s most decorated champions. 


In the intervening years the Empire of Light only grew in its radiance, spreading the domain of The Steed further and further, using the power of War Priests to both find and open pathways to other planets worthy of great battle, and eventually conquer. In this The Empire of Light has pleased The Steed, and as a result has grown in power, each vassal world or state only adding to the Empire’s homeland. The warriors of each conquered people who accept the truth of The Steed have found honored positions in the Legions, and those who are less strong, but bask in The Steed’s radiance have also found their place providing for the needs of the Empire. The rest, failing to see the radiance The Steed offers, have been lost to history, as they deserve. 


Sixty-four years ago, during one of its many crusades, the Empire of Light happened upon a group of demonic creatures it had never seen before - creatures of Stone and Fire, who possessed weapons no Legion had ever faced before. The Legions facing these Demons fell, and the Empire of Light rejoiced, for it had found an opponent worthy of its might. These Demons, The Steed revealed, were a test from The Steed for its most favored children. The Steed declared, as always through its War Clerics, that when the Empire bested these foes, they would ascend to even greater heights.


In this endeavor, however, the glorious Empire struggled, making little progress against the Demons - proving triumphant in only one of its battles. This war lasted fifty-three years. To fail in war is to fail The Steed, the most glorious and gracious of gods. But even The Steed’s grace has its limits, and The Steed, realizing that his children would be unable to best this challenge, did as all good parents do, and taught his children a much needed lesson. 


One morning, the great Citadel of Conquest, where War Clerics and Magus maintained the pathways to their vassal lands, suddenly went quiet, and the Blood Angel Mathinius - who maintained the Citadel - left to join the side of The Steed. And then, six days later, the portals opened back up, but behind them were not the worlds the Empire of Light knew, but battlefields of Radiant Blood - the Eternal Battlefield that all great soldiers were promised when they died. And from them poured the honored dead, souls of departed heroes, who knew nothing but the perfection in war - and with their spears pointed at the Empire itself. Thus started one of the most brutal tests The Steed had ever proffered, with The Empire’s full might tested as they attempted to drive back the hordes of honored dead. Just under a year later, The Steed, knowing that his children had grown in strength from his lesson, closed the gateways of the honored dead, and the Empire routed the remaining souls, who returned to their glorious resting place. The people of the Empire also learned a second valuable lesson - the gifts granted by The Steed are only granted because they are worthy of Its Radiance - and that can be taken away if they slack in their duties.


But The Steed, gracious and benevolent, opened another door of opportunity for its people, for as the portals to the Eternal Battlefield closed, a massive tree appeared in the distance, towering over the Empire. Distant lands within the Empire’s domain were deemed too weak by The Steed to continue, and disappeared overnight, being replaced with foreign lands and foreign people. This Tree, the Wellspring, was The Steed’s greatest gift to its people - an eternal paradise. This, like all things, must be earned, and The Steed additionally provided a new test - great civilizations of tremendous power now bordered its lands, people who were not there before, and those people would need to be defeated so the Empire could ascend its final test. What is more, there now exists a great gate at the bottom of the Wellspring, guarded by Demons - the same test The Steed gave their children before. Those Demons were the final test, to be surmounted after The Empire of Light conquered the other peoples and absorbed them into Its Radiance. 


And so the Empire of Light’s last chapter unfolded like its first - in glorious bloodshed. The Empire of Light rapidly expanded its lands around the Wellspring, quickly absorbing and conquering the nations in its immediate vicinity - many of which were struggling with their own issues. Their initial surge was easily won, but eventually slowed as it first encountered significant resistance in a nearby mountain range - a range occupied by The People of Silver, and secondly by the smaller nations who began hearing of The Empire of Light’s rampage, and who banded together to try to stop them. The Empire of Light, however, is inevitable, and while their tide slowed, it never stopped, and all nations inhabiting the new terrain of the Wellspring braced for eventual conquest. 


This war culminated in W7, with the Crimson Dawn - a massive offensive spearheaded by the Empress’s Legions, that seemed poised to wipe out remaining local resistance. It was during this offensive that The Empire of Light first encountered and clashed directly with The Unified Technarchy. The Unified Technarchy, having learned early of their new enemies and prepared specifically for them, offered some token resistance, but not enough to stop them. The Crimson Dawn liberated lands for the Empire as far as the Great Gate at the base of Wellspring - where the Wellspring Demons kept a silent vigil. The Empire of Light took this victory - the liberation of the Gates of Paradise from the unworthy - as a moment to regroup and strategize, and quickly built a citadel outside the Great Gates, named Paradise’s Flame. The rest of the civilizations prepared for the next stage of the offensive, and plotted their cowardly plans. 


And then, late one night in W9 the Empire was struck hard by their cowardly enemies who were too weak to fight in glorious combat. The Unified Technarchy managed to infiltrate the Citadel of Paradise’s Flame and strike a blow that killed Asmenathus - the blood angel responsible for the greatest of the Empire’s fortresses. This treachery left Paradise’s Flame open to attack, and the cowardly forces launched an assault, routing the Citadel’s defenders and pushing them in bloody battle almost to the foot of the Gates of Paradise. And then the worst happened - the massive Demons of the Gates ended their silent vigil and awoke, laying waste not only to Paradise’s Flame and the Empire’s Legions, but also the routing force. Thousands lost in a matter of hours - and not lost to glorious combat, lost to cowardice and treachery. 


All nations felt this loss. In particular the armies representing the barely allied smaller nations saw almost all of their remaining standing military wiped out overnight. Those nations shortly went on to be absorbed by either the Empire of Light, or the Technarchy, as they had little left to defend themselves. 


The Empire of Light has since regrouped and began preparing for the next offensive, the next war, albeit with significantly reduced resources. Difficult decisions are being made as to the next move - it is clear that The Steed must be appeased - for defeat cannot be known by its children for long. Rumors are already spreading of some of the Blood Angels becoming irate with the Empire’s people, killing their own attendants in some cases, and going missing in others. Whispers can be heard that this is just the beginning of the punishment The Steed has in store for their failure. 


And then a new opportunity presented itself for the Empire of Light, and for its enemies. A new golden tree appeared - looking just like the Wellspring itself, and conveniently placed on the borders of Empire territory. A new test, the Empire has determined, a test that this time they will not fail. This new land houses the keys to paradise, and it must be brought into The Steed’s Radiance. 
Social/Political Structure
The Empire is structured into War Houses, all of which field legions to be used at the Empire of Light’s discretion. When those legions are victorious in conquest, they retain a portion of the conquered population and lands - and thus grow in influence. The direct rule of the Empire and its resources is controlled by the Emperor/Empress - the most favored by The Steed, as dictated through his Church of Light. This great honor is bestowed upon the house with the greatest military service and strength. For 156 years this has been the House Brussaunt, including four (three after the fall of Paradise’s Flame) blood angels that operate within its territory and respond to their war horns. The next strongest house, House Maison, has two blood angels who respond to their horns, and around two thirds of the standing army. The Empress - Adaline Brussaunt - rules with an iron fist, and her perpetual rule is almost assumed. 


Socially, status is heavily decided by the martial power of the house in question. All houses focus the majority of their efforts on building their martial power. Some houses rise in status above their rank by specializing in specific types of warfare, and then pledging to a greater house, who in turn raises their position by proxy.


For individuals, status is determined by martial prowess - with war makers enjoying the top of the social hierarchy. Laborers are in a much lower social caste, and while they are allowed lives with some fulfillment and freedom, they retain far fewer rights and privileges than those of the war makers. While the social system is very strict within these boundaries, movement between castes happens frequently. It is not unusual for a farmer's son or daughter to dedicate themselves to physical perfection and be given a seat in the feasting halls next to their comrades. When a person is deemed a worthy warrior, acceptance is quick, and histories are forgotten in the brotherhood of war - for this is what The Steed demands. 


People pairing and raising families is common - but the bonds between battle-siblings are the tightest. Often these relationships are more than platonic, and this expanded relationship between battle brothers and sisters is often in addition to hearth-families, who are more focused on the raising of children, and the creation of new warriors. This is considered normal, and even encouraged. 


Elderly laborers help with child rearing of the laborer caste. When old age prevents any productive work, it is societally considered polite for them to leave the home and enter the wilderness and never return. Elderly warrior caste members similarly focus on training, and once their age progresses far enough they join a Legion in a Glory Campaign, where they are often used as first-wave shock troopers. It's considered a great honor to have your last moments in battle, and considered distasteful to wait so long for your Glory Campaign that you are unable to allow the enemy to earn your death. 
Military Organization
The War Houses of The Empire report directly to the Empress. Each War House is led by a Primus who commands multiple Legions each headed by their own Legate. Each Legate commands a group of Centurions, who in turn command a group of Captains, who each lead a unit of 10 Soldiers.


Within a Legion there will also be Specialist Units with Captains that report separately to the Legate.
Religion
Religion in the Empire of Light is simple - bask in the Radiance of The Steed, or be put to the sword. The Steed does not suffer idols besides itself. It is not uncommon that people are rooted out by Inquisitors of Light for unworthy idol worship and publicly put to the sword. Worship of the Steed is found across all houses, who call for its strength in war. More formal worship happens from the War Clerics, who form a Church of Light in service to The Steed directly. It is said their High Cleric speaks directly with The Steed on occasion, and is often in direct communication with the Blood Angels. The High Cleric, and by commandment the High Inquisitor, oversees investigations of heresy within the Empire that, while rare, are to be rooted out and punished quickly. The High Cleric also is often called by the Empress to adjudicate disputes between houses, and to advise on the will of The Steed. Often these matters are resolved via holy combat. The High Cleric’s word is absolute, and they represent the second arm of The Steed, alongside the Empress herself. 


Ever since the fall of Paradise’s Flame, clerics and worshipers of The Steed have felt an aching emptiness in the spot that The Steed’s Radiance used to fill. They can still feel The Steed’s power radiate through them - and they still have access to The Steed’s divine gifts - but it’s as if The Steed is giving them the silent treatment. The only time The Steed’s children have ever felt something akin to this was shortly before The Steed Enlightened his children and opened the gates to the Eternal Battlefield. 


The Steed blesses its deserving children after death with the Eternal Battlefield, a land where its soldiers will only ever know eternal battle, and inexhaustible mead and feasting. Here you transcend your physical form and never tire, allowing you to enjoy the glory of battle for eternity. The greatest of these warriors may be allowed to serve The Steed as a Blood Angel, or as a Steel Spirit (worthy spirits bound into armaments of war to perform a wide variety of tasks). 
Streams in Silver
The people who inhabit the Summit of the Great Mare, also called the People of Silver are considered some of the chief enemies of the Empire of Light. The mountain range exists squarely within the Empire of Light’s territory, and their people remaining unconquered is a great insult to the Empire. Heretic propaganda reports that people can flee to their halls for safety, but this is a deception so they can feed the once-faithful to their bloody coward god. 
The Blood Forge Sabotage
Seven months ago The Blood Forge, a citadel dedicated to The Steed situated in the Empire Heartland was sabotaged, exploding in flame and destroying the facility. The Empire has traced this cowardly act to a group of Technarchy spies who had just recently infiltrated the Heartland. The Empire firmly denies reports that Empire soldiers themselves were involved. The Empire further reassures its people that the recent Inquisitor Interrogations are being done as a routine safety matter, and if any minor traitorous elements are discovered, they will be handled swiftly and brutally. 
Auros 
It has been determined that the Golden Oak, the name of the tree at the center of Auros, is a gift from The Steed themselves. However, The Steed’s duplicitous enemies - The Technarchy - are very well poised to mobilize in any effort to take the tree directly. This has put the Empire of Light in a difficult position - a true test to understanding complete war - either invade Auros directly and risk the Tree directly, or speak to the Auros people and help them understand the treachery of the Technarchy, and the benevolence of The Steed, and soften the lands for annexation. This has led to a tentative and rapidly fraying local cease-fire with the Technarchy while they prepare for conversations with Auros directly. 
Technology Level
High Fantasy/Magic mixed with over-the-top martial worship. Some of the gifts given by The Steed through its Blood Angels resembles technology in function, but all technological output looks like over-the-top fantasy objects. Massive cities and citadels forged by Blood Angels, all in worship to The Steed. Statues/architecture heavily features horse-themes. Standard is of a sun, decorations to this standard include horse motifs.
Aesthetic
Over the top hyper-militaristic clothing, often incorporating elements of armor. Referentially, look at Warhammer 40K’s The Empire of Man, and give everything a more fantasy edge, and you have the Empire of Light. Reds, Yellows, Golds, Greys, and Whites make up a common color palette. Horse motifs, and iconography of The Steed are very common. Elegant gowns and stiff suits are common during formal events, as are military uniforms - no matter the occasion, however, each dress or outfit includes a weapon of some kind as a part of the outfit. 
Crime in the Empire
What the Empire of Light considers a crime differs greatly from the other cultures that live around the Wellspring. Punishments for crimes such as theft, cowardice, civil disobedience, and trade with other civilizations are swift and severe. Goods from other societies are largely considered inferior, so black markets are not common. Violence within reason is largely decriminalized - formal dueling and informal attacking as a response to insult are widely accepted, and in interpersonal disputes, the courts often rely on trial by combat.


Miscellaneous Lore
The Five
The Five were a team of warriors that perished in the Treachery of Paradise’s Flame - save for Landry Brussaunt, who managed to lead a heroic charge and break away with the Legion’s remaining forces. Many of their martial techniques are taught across the empire, and many looked up to them as beacons of martial perfection. 


Members of the Five:
      * Amelie Maison. Largely considered the leader of the Five, Amelie was a brilliant battlefield tactician, and one of the most renowned shield fighters the Empire has ever seen.
      * Armand Floriot. While all of the Five walked in Its Radiance and were loved by The Steed, Armand was the most devout, serving as a divine champion directly.
      * Elodie St-Cyr. Known for her fighting style that mixed blade and magic. Sometimes called The Earth Cleaver for her ritual that swallowed an opposing army into the ground.
      * Landry Brussaunt. Known as The Never-Broken, Landry has been said to have never fallen in battle, and is rumored to be able to shrug off blows that would kill anyone else. Landry is the younger brother of The Empress herself.
      * Celeste Artois. Specialized in ranged combat, Celeste was looked down on in their early career. The massive wake of bodies they left behind quickly silenced their critics.
Common Expressions/Holidays
Expressions are not exhaustive - they are meant to give a vibe and baseline.

      * “Fire of the Five”: Used to describe particularly inspired acts of martial prowess.
      * “Coward”: Calling someone a Coward in the Empire of Light is the greatest of offenses, and demands blood in return. Being called a coward and not answering with steel is considered pathetic.
      * “Cask that One”: Used similarly to “they are a keeper”, and represents personal attraction or admiration towards a person. Refers to people given the honor to rest within Berserker Casks to be used as living weapons. Berserker Casks, or simply Casking, is a ritual where a particularly talented warrior is marked with sigils and spells and augments of all houses, and then entombed in an eternal cask that preserves their augmentations from fading, but prevents full sleep. The Cask is only opened at the sites of great battles, where the Berserker is unleashed upon its foes. They are then re-entombed upon victory - for never fear, the Berserker’s heart is always at peace. Considered a great honor. 
      * Enlightenment Day: Celebrated in Fall, Enlightenment day commemorates the day that the Citadel of Conquest’s halls went silent, and the Honored Dead spilled from its portals. It is a day of reverence and awe for The Steed’s power, glory, and teachings. Tournaments ring out across the empire, with even the non-warrior casts participating in serf-brawls.
Naming Conventions


Names in the Empire of Light are sometimes hard to pronounce for those not used to the throaty sounds they employ. Often with overemphasized syllables in some parts and underemphasized in others, it’s a random guess sometimes when attempting to read a written name for non-natives to the empire. Often remedial classes will be held for new citizens to culture their heathen mouths.


First Names:
Adaline, Melloran, Oeurphant, Aurne, Norne, Colleur, Amandenra, Kielfreu


Houses:
Bruissant, Maison, Alauren, Feurissen, Millaurne


________________


The Shorn
The Shorn


Synopsis
The Shorn are a militant society seeking to free all peoples from the tyranny of divinity. After a century of being forced to slaughter each other, a group across their two societies, the Wilders and People of the City banded together and developed weaponry capable of killing their gods. The Godhunters took the power of the divine into their weapons and themselves, and now lead the Shorn in assisting other civilizations with breaking their shackles.


Upon arriving at the Wellspring, the city enchantments that handled the Shorn’s production of food, water, and sanitation started to fail, endangering their way of life. The Shorn now find themselves relocating further and further inland, recently taking up refuge in a nearby cave system, where they have been slowly rebuilding. Their new neighbors, a civilization of insectoid formians, did not appreciate their resettlement, and the Shorn were locked in a brief but bloody war that saw the Formian god dead, and the Shorn as the sole occupants of their new home. The Shorn’s problems, however, are still not over - as the Empire has turned their gaze towards them, and adjustment to their new life has been filled with trials. 

[Be sure to read the , and the The Main Camera of the Story section when considering playing a character from The Shorn. Reach out to staff if you need help developing a character.]
Lineage Population
Common: Aewen, Chimera, Human, Ogrim 
Rare: Forged, Lost, Oaksworn, Underkin
The Legend
Long ago there were tribes of people making lives on their small planet. Life was hard for these tribes but they made do. They fought the wilds and each other for resources. Their societies venerated the strong and self-sufficient, and from their bloodshed rose the ultimate warrior, one who wielded the biggest stick and kept peace through assured retribution, the God of Wrath and Mercy, Kildath.


Kildath’s followers warred with each other, but through ritual combat and strong codes of honor, did their best to limit loss of life. Territories changed hands, generations-long feuds developed, and the people were full of hot emotion. They fought hard, they partied hard and loved even harder. Their love and bonds attracted the essence of a comet passing by, who came down and manifested into the goddess Filia, who took Kildath (along with many mortals) as her lover.

Weaker tribes were pushed further and further out into less resource rich lands. They grew to be craftier and grew closer together in order to survive, coming up with all kinds of tools and inventions to make their lives on the fringes easier. One day, a scouting party came across a large cave inside which they found a giant sleeping. The giant had vast resource stockpiles and intricate machines the likes of which they had never seen before. They sought to take these relics, to tinker and fiddle until they could understand their workings, but as they turned to leave, haul in hand, their eyes caught a sea of stars painted upon the giant's wall. So struck with awe, they did not notice the giant’s waking until he was upon them. He raged and beat at them, furious that they were taking his tools until he looked at what had distracted his burgling pests, his paintings of the night sky and the beautiful comet he admired so much. Seeing that these small creatures understood and marvelled at his creations he calmed down, nursed the people back to health and taught them what he knew of Art and the Arcane.


The followers of Castor used their newfound mastery of the arcane to mould the land to suit their needs, but the disparate Arcanists had different visions for the land, and individually wrote different runes, leading to a clash in the flow of the arcane, and the land bent into fractal patterns and the Arcane wove itself into knots. The concentration of chaos created an equal and opposite force, a concentration of order. This accumulation of order immediately gave itself a recognizable form, a severe woman clad in golden robes who went to each of the Arcane structures that brought about this knot of chaos in turn and altered them, resolving the land into a lush and structured paradise. This force of Order named herself Dominica, and laid claim to the land and the people that lived there. She promised that the peoples of the outlands could lead lives of plenty and peace if they followed her instructions, and so they did.


Castor came out of his cave and saw the transformed landscape around him, he stumbled into the vaunted palace with glorious white walls at the heart of the city and wondered at who had created it. Dominica manifested before him, claiming responsibility for her city and chastised the Giant for giving such powerful tools to the hands of mortals. Castor refused to apologize, for he said that if her genesis was the end result, then he would scour the stars and grant the Arcane to all creatures in the hope that it would create even one more perfect being like her. Dominica was so moved by Castor’s admiration that she took him, too, as her own. 


The Outlanders, now the Peoples of the City, performed the work necessary to meet the needs of their society. Clean water flowed in every home, waste was disposed of, food was grown in their fields in neat rows and fed the city with plenty to store in case of emergency. It barely even took a half-day for each to earn their keep. With their ample free time the people turned to imitate Castor and learn the arts of music, poetry, painting, and sculpture, and filled their city with many wonders to behold.


As the city grew, the warrior-clans living in the heartland began to see the edges of it from their halls at the top of their hills. Curious, they sent warbands in to scout the city and, upon seeing the vast wealth of land, set about to drive the People of the City from their fields. Divine light shone down as Dominica appeared and turned the invaders to stone. Enraged at the loss of his warriors, Kildath possessed the body of the strongest of the warband and broke from their stone prison, engaging Dominica in a brutal conflict that lasted for days. 


On the eve of the 6th day, Dominica dealt a decisive blow, striking Kildath and the land he stood on ten feet low, fracturing the continent marking a dividing line between the territory of the City and what she proclaimed “The Wilds”, from now on to be physically lower than her City, to be lesser. Dominica then knelt, clearly exhausted by her exertion. Castor came out from behind the group of onlookers and helped her up. He turned to take her back to the palace, but saw that below there was a figure who he recognized doing the same for Kildath. He knew Filia in any form she took and his heart ached to see the Star-child. She gave him a fond, but sad smile as she dragged the body of her champion and lover back to their home.


It was only a brief interaction, but Filia’s image stuck in Castor’s head as his desire for her grew ever greater. In secret, Castor furiously created images of Filia in order to express his feelings. This strengthened the bond between them until she could ignore it no longer, and she disguised herself as a City-woman and made her way to him deep in the city’s catacombs. With one look, Castor collapsed into Filia’s arms and they became as one.


For years, they met in secret and peace was had between the City and the Wilds. The gods had children; Filia gave birth to Dorati, goddess of Joy and Mischief and Rokka, goddess of Sport and Strength – Dominica gave birth to Illumin, god of Insight and Wisdom and Ennar the god of Craftsmanship.


Dorati felt drawn to the City, ignoring the edicts separating it and the Wilds, she would slip through and play pranks upon the adults and games with the children. Illumin, curious about the wild child, used his gift to predict where she would show up. Upon seeing her, he knew that she was Castor’s daughter, recognizing that they made the same faces when pranking and creating. Struck with an unbearable truth to the prudish City people, he ran to the palace and told his mother.


Dominica, filled with righteous fury, descended into the wilds and tore Filia’s lovers off of her and beat her bloody for daring to touch what was hers. Filia, desperate to survive, drew on her bonds and summoned the whole of the wilds to defend her, thrashing Dominica, so far from her seat of power, and nearly killing her. Castor appeared in a flash of Arcane and pulled Dominica back to the City to heal.

Dominica, marred and made imperfect by the experience, bade Castor to make weapons of war to march upon the wilds, to conquer them once and for all and add them to the City. Castor reluctantly obeyed and Dominica fueled her people with her righteous indignation and sent them into the wilds, butchering their warriors. 


Filia sped up the gestation and maturation of their peoples to keep them in the fight. Kildath fought bravely to fell a platoon of City folk from whom Dorati scavenged Arcane energy, arming the Wilds with weapons to equal the City.


Dominica and Castor countered by creating constructs that housed the minds of the people of the City to fight minimizing loss of life.


For 100 years the City and the Wilds fought a bloody and unwinnable war, churning through humans like cattle in a slaughterhouse. Each side grew better and better at unleashing horrific punishment on their opposition. 
The Liberation
Life was brutal and short for the Wilds, and stifling and controlled for the City. The people suffered at the hands of the gods, filled with their fervor and hatred for each other, living and dying for a conflict they did not make, for gods that no longer saw them as anything but tools to enact vengeance upon each other.


50 years ago, a group of Arcanists and Warriors worked in secret to craft a weapon to free the people of the gods, making the first god killing weapon, a spear, used in battle to fell Illumin, son of Dominica. Armed with power siphoned from the god, Charsin Adamant led the Godhunters to take down each of the gods in turn and absorb their power, uniting the peoples of the Wilds and the City to become one people, finally freed from the tyranny of the gods, now calling themselves the Shorn.


The Godhunters - Charsin Adamant, Dorne Weaver, Mille Wallkin, Filian Hawker, Senri Ashkeeper, Aphara Deo and Warden Faste, those who slew and took the power of Illumin, Dominica, Castor, Filia, Kildath, Rokka and Ennar respectively - then turned their sight to the stars, seeking to free others of the shackles of the divine. They opened portals to areas of great divine energy, sensed through their weapons, eager to shed more god blood.


11 years ago, after a bloody campaign across the universe during which Aphara Deo and Warden Faste tragically met their ends, the Shorn were forced to pull back due to a local issue where the food in their land could no longer nourish the bodies of their people.  The Godhunters puzzled over what was causing this ailment, eventually discovering that it was due to a force growing in the universe that had spread into their lands, the Void.


Desperate to save their people, Charsin led the Godhunters to perform a rite of transposition to move their land to a section of the universe away from the Void. The rite succeeded but at great cost. Charsin was overwhelmed by the Arcane and destroyed, and many of the Shorn did not survive the transition, with vast swaths of land and people being torn asunder.


The remaining Godhunters and Shorn grieved the loss of their people and leader, but could not stop for they found themselves in a different world – great holes in the ground opened up and led underground into depths unknown, vast mountains and forests were visible on the horizon that had not been there before, as well as a massive golden glowing tree in the center of their land. It was as if their City had been picked up and placed somewhere else. Most disturbingly, in view at the edge from their tower’s vantage point they could see that the meadows and farmland transitioned abruptly into an ashen gray land, as if from that point forward all light and life had been violently sucked out of the very earth itself. The Void had followed them here.


After some time spent acclimating to the new land and tending to their people, reports started coming in of enchantments gone awry, previous City sigilwork that had been stable for a hundred years warping and endangering their citizens. The Godhunters made the call to evacuate the city, moving their populace to a nearby cave system for shelter until new temporary housing structures could be built and ultimately the Arcanists could figure out what went wrong with the City’s sigils and fix them.


7 years ago, Filian and Mille took a squadron to scout the land for resources and other inhabitants to get the lay of the land. While the diplomats scouted, Dorne and Senri set about fortifying their position. When the finishing touches were being made to their encampment, a horde of insectoid warriors poured out of the cave they set their base against.


Dorne and Senri fought valiantly to protect their people, and found that their foes bore the mark of Divinity. Eager to free their enemies of their god, so that they might join in their number free from the leash, Dorne and Senri with their elite warriors and arcanists pushed deep into the cavernous tunnels below and fought the monstrous beast that held the insects in thrall – slaying their foe and elevating a new warrior to the rank of Godhunter, Kelkar Linebreaker. The newest addition to the Godhunters has rarely been seen since elevation, and rumors have it that they’re undertaking highly dangerous missions in enemy territory with a small elite team.

The Formian people, now freed from their shackles, joined with the Shorn and welcomed them into their caverns teaching them how to navigate quickly under the earth.


Filian and Mille returned with word of where resources could be found as well as news on who their new neighbors were. They discovered warlords pillaging the land for their war god – high artificers attempting to steal the lightning from the sky to power their artificial mind – a peaceful but territorial people of the woods who worshipped the mighty Oak who their legends say placed the stars in the sky – grown adults who play games with their lives and record them on arcane devices  – and smith-warriors who hammer to the beat of an unheard song and create artifice-level weapons and armor.

Dorne and Senri listened to Mille and Filian describe the land they were in, rich with gods to hunt, but upon suggesting they continue their campaign Mille and Filian were staunchly against it. They wished to ensure that the City was reclaimed first, and thought that the Shorn should first bide their time and shore up their strength, and only then continue their hunt. 

As a compromise, they would spread word of the fall of the tyrant-god of the Formians and threaten the same fate for any agent of the divine who would dare set foot on their lands and make overtures of peace to their citizens by trading their Sigilcraft for knowledge and resources from the other factions.


4 years ago, The Empire of Light did not take kindly to their threats and sent a legion into their lands. The Shorn retreated into the tunnels beneath and fought fiercely with guerilla tactics, slaying the legion and even killed one of the Empire’s Blood Angels.


The Empire, embroiled in other conflicts, did not attack again. They said that the Shorn could “keep their sliver of land near the edge of nowhere, for now”. Since then the Shorn have set themselves the task of reclaiming their City.


Recently, however, a bright beam of light heralded a new arrival to the Wellspring, a second, smaller tree that bore an uncanny similarity to the glowing tree at the center. Curious about the new arrivals, and being relatively close, the Godhunters have sent their diplomats to extend the same message they had given the others. One of peace and, if necessary, promised retribution.
The People
The Shorn exhibit traits from both of their origin cultures, the City and the Wilds. Children are reared communally and are not considered the property of their parents. Caregivers are tasked with encouraging children into honing skills that contribute to society in some meaningful fashion and pursue passions and works of art in their free time. 


During puberty (6 for Aewen, 15 for Underkin and 13 for other lineages), those that exhibit martial prowess are encouraged to undertake The Proving, a series of physical and arcane challenges that screens for those with aptitude to join the military. Children train vigorously for The Proving in the hopes of one day wielding a god killing weapon and joining the Godhunters.


Those that fail The Proving or that opt out are sorted in accordance with the needs of the Shorn and trained to fill their societal role, regardless of their own preferences or proclivities.


Some of the older generation follow the tenets of the dead gods and even worship some of the Godhunters as new incarnations of the fallen deities. Such behavior is taboo, and performed in secret.
Military Organization


There are two branches of the military that report to the Godhunter Council, the Warrior branch and the Arcanist branch.


For the most part, these branches act independently, however in mixed deployment, the following order of seniority applies:


Councillor
Architect (A3)
Commander (W4)
Weaver (A2)
Captain (W3)
Scribe (A1)
Soldier (W2)
Recruit (W1)


When engaging the Divine, the Godhunters muster the most skilled warriors and arcanists of the Shorn into a force called the Vanguard. While members of the Vanguard are not given a formal rank or title, inclusion in any deployment is considered to be the highest military honor.
The Creed
While religious dogma has been abolished, the Shorn have taken pieces of wisdom hidden in scripture and assembled them into The Creed, a series of instructions for how to live a fulfilling life, finding purpose and supporting your fellow man, as well as practical advice for avoiding arcane mishaps, tending to the land properly and maintaining a healthy body.
Godhunter Base Incantation
Shorn "clerics" who follow the teachings of a Godhunter use a unique incantation, rather than the one listed under the Cleric class:


"I call my will to join the fight, I wield and shape creation's light,
With praise to those whom I admire, I summon now my inner fire,
From Godhunters, my power's source, with grit and hope I give it force,
As killers of the fell divine, I claim the power that is mine..."
Their Works
The Shorn have a robust runic system to command the Arcane. Arcanists prepare spells by weaving arcane sigils into prewritten scrolls, tattoos or clothing and channeling mana into them to produce any manner of effect. Writing new spell sigils is the work of a lifetime, leaning heavily on the achievements of the previous generation. 


The vast majority of labor required of The Shorn is maintaining the sigil work that provides for the people, recording and backing up sigils and training new Arcanists in the art.


Occasionally Sigils will need to be modified to deal with new variables and changes to the environment. This undertaking can lead to unintended and disastrous side effects and so is only undertaken by the most senior of Sigil crafters.
Aesthetic
Modern Shornish military dress is cloth/linen robes ending at the knee or mid-calf of tan, brown and black in order of ascending seniority with red hemming/accents. Arcanists and Sigilcrafters wear black with white runes [recommend glow in the dark paint].


Armor of any kind or style may be worn over these robes, and often display a degree of individualism, with some Wilders choosing their traditional bone / hide armor to display pride in their heritage and City folk wearing their traditional bronze armor.


Civilians will often wear grey or unaccented tans / browns in their working capacity, though exceptional individuals may get away with more colorful displays depending on their power and influence.


In informal and private settings, some Shornish will wear their traditional garb for their origin culture.


Traditional garb of the City is brightly colored robes, with the elite wearing white with gold and green accents. Traditional garb for the Wilders is furs and hides and bone over linens.


Crime Among the Shorn
Smuggling and black markets exist similarly as within other cultures, especially since their evacuation from their City, but the Shorn's unique reputation has also made them the most common group to approach when an unscrupulous buyer is looking to purchase another individual's death. How often (if ever) they accept such offers is not well known. Since religion within Shorn society is strictly illegal, the most commonly traded contraband, more than food or weapons, is holy symbols and religious texts. Information about the construction of their god-killing weapons is also in high demand across the Wellspring, but so far no other civilization has been able to replicate them.
Funerary Rites
Traditionally, Wilders burn their dead atop pyres of their possessions and Urbanites will bury their dead after wrapping them in cloth scribed with farewell messages. Now, in the Wellspring, circumstances sometimes require a summary burial or burning with less fanfare and ritual.
Common Expressions/Holidays
Expressions are not exhaustive - they are meant to give a vibe and baseline.


      * “Ten Feet Low”: Used as a threat “I’ll strike you ten feet low” or as an expression of utter despair. “Here I lay, ten feet low.” Interpreted by some of the older generations as a slur against the Wildfolk.
      * “Debts Owed, Debts Paid” - Promise of retribution or a promise of favor for favor.
      * “By my bonds, I Swear” - An oath tying your word to the word of your family and friends, that you would sooner disgrace them than break this oath.
      * “Domming” - Commonly used when hogging something. “Quit domming the steak”. References Dominica, the now dead goddess of Order, in disdain. 
      * The Proving - Traditionally held with the first flowers that bloom after the frost, the Proving is a series of challenges that determines eligibility for the Military. The first round is a tournament of unrestrained might. Contestants are paired off and those with three wins and no losses immediately pass. 


In the second round, those remaining are randomly divided into groups and their wounds are healed to the point of not being life threatening. Each group is assigned a wild hunt target and may use whatever skills they possess to bring it low.  If any in the group die or are left behind, the whole group fails this challenge. Successful hunting squads are inducted with a vote of confidence requiring a majority of the squad to vet your contribution to the hunt.


Those remaining may choose to fail the proving, or undertake the most dangerous trial, that of the battlemage. Aspirants are required to face an Arcanoloth, the result of magic gone haywire and attempt to resolve its spellwork. Arcanoloths, being creatures of pure magic cannot be felled by any means but unweaving the complex sigil work that caused them to come into existence, which cannot be attempted without putting one's own life in danger, as the magic of the Arcanoloth can travel back through one’s sigil work into their body.
Naming Conventions
Shornish surnames typically come from self or others as a distinguisher amongst first names, often as descriptors of jobs or demeanor. First names are a hope for the life of the child. A wish for a soft, laidback life will have sing-song names, where hope for a bold and challenging life is hard consonants and abrupt stops.

Examples:
Tender, Weaver, Nitpicker, Ashkeeper, Fortsitter, Cacher, Bold, Adamant, Stern

Soft, Laidback:
Maude, Fleur, Illyan, Liara, Aime, Adrian, Dielle, Jian


Bold, Challenging:
Karen, Dirk, Brooke, Hawke, Kite, Kait, Warden




________________


Streams in Silver
Streams in Silver


Synopsis
