Occupying a mountain range in the middle of lands found within the Empire of Light, Streams in Silver, and the People of Silver, find themselves besieged on all sides, with allies hard to come by. The nearest piece of non-empire land to the Krish mountain range is one hundred miles to the west, so regular external support fundamentally does not exist. But what the sky forgets, the forge supplies - the Song in Iron still sings through the People’s forges and the barricades still hold.  


As if these trying times did not temper the steel enough, when not fighting against the empire’s soldiers, huffing and puffing up their mountain, they are fighting a more insidious opponent - the Krushkar, a race of insectoid-like creatures that swarm the lower halls like gnats in a fire. The song-halls still remember the Great Swarm, and the People of Silver are not content to sit on their hands and wait for the anvil to drop.


[Be sure to read the , and the The Main Camera of the Story section when considering playing a character from Streams in Silver. Reach out to staff if you need help developing a character.]
Lineage Population
Common: Underkin, Forged, Human, Ogrim
Rare: Aewen, Chimera, Lost 
Very Rare: Oaksworn
History
Hearty and headstrong, the People of Silver long ago settled in the great mountains of Krish - fleeing the mainland of their world as the Old Forge-Smoke of their predecessors began choking the land. There they turned what was thought to be an isolated desolate mountain range into a beautiful metropolis built deep in the stone. The mountain became their protector and their provider, and they prospered greatly.


And then, when expanding deeper to build the grand new mining city of Glitters of Stars they awoke something - the Krushkar - a race of insectoid creatures who could not be reasoned with, who knew only blood, whose mouths dripped with acid. The Krushkar flooded the city-to-be, and began skittering towards cities further and further up. The People of Silver made the Krushkar pay for every inch of tunnel they took - but the Krushkar’s endless swarm happily paid in full. 


As the smiths-people furiously tried to forge a future in their craft halls they heard something new - a Song in their Iron that wasn’t there before. Those who focused on the song connected with something greater - and the Song taught their smiths well. The armaments produced with the Song were blessed things, and helped the people of Silver push back the hives for long enough to build border fortifications and dig in.


The swarms eventually abated, but continued to pose a constant threat. Still, a threat was better than the desolation tomorrow used to promise, and the People began to recover. Occasionally, The Song would grow louder in warning, and the Krushkar would Swarm, mounting in a brief but bloody offensive that would see the People’s battlements pushed to the breaking point. But despite this they persisted. 


And then, 11 years ago, the people of Silver felt the Song grow weak, and the Swarm surged to a level never seen before. Not only were the normal areas attacked, but the Swarm managed to dig paths to the surface, and new places that had only known cold coal were attacked. The People of Silver were pushed from every angle, and without the power of the Song singing in their forges, despite fighting with every ounce of courage they had, began to crumble. This bloody struggle lasted a year and twelve days. 


A year after the Great Swarm arose, the three greatest smiths heard The Song return with a clarity that had never been heard before - the notes so clear and strong to them that they were almost driven mad. They furiously forged armaments day and night, and three days later seven of the people of Silver’s greatest warriors were called upon by the Song, and given peerless weapons with unprecedented power. It is told that in that moment the Song sang a story heard only by them, and they set off, alone, towards the deepest part of the mountain. Nine days later the Great Swarm dissipated, their chitin easily crushed as their minds seemed to wander. The Song’s Chosen, as they are now called, never returned - and deeper in the caverns the Swarm still wanders, seemingly without purpose, making efforts to find the Chosen futile.


Before the People of Silver relit their forges, yet another thing drew their attention. As their armies reclaimed the surface, they found that the lands surrounding their mountain had changed, revealing locales unfamiliar to them. Even more striking was a massive golden tree that now inhabited the horizon, its size making even their mountains look small. 


Shortly after, soldiers bearing an emblem of the sun came up their mountain, huffing and puffing like old bellows. Cordial at first, talks quickly turned into demands and the Empire of Light, as they called themselves, issued a declaration that the People of Silver were now a vassal of their empire, and that they needed to turn over their priests and idols. 


The People of Silver, no strangers to war, and knowing their border forts like the backs of their hands, returned the envoy’s demands with blood, and war quickly ensued. 


The Empire and the People of Silver have been embroiled in a bitter war. But swarms still occur in their lower halls, and while they have managed to keep the Empire at bay for these 10 years, many fear for how long their opposition can last.


Bolstering them, however, are many refugees from the Empire’s lands, who have come seeking shelter and escape from the Empire’s yoke. This has further enraged the Empire, which has dedicated more and more resources towards rooting out The People with every passing season. This support has allowed the People of Silver to continue to man their battlements and enriched their techniques. To that end the People of Silver have helped those who seek escape from the Empire find it with the People - so long as they are willing to be shaped by the Song’s cast. But the Empire has resources and scale that far outstrips The People of Silver, and this war cannot be stalled forever.


Supplies dwindle though, and the Empire is becoming more sophisticated - even going so far as finding ways under the surface through tunnels made and then forgotten during the Great Swarm. Allies are sorely needed, but hard to come by. Recently, rumor has it the Empire is preoccupied with some new gem to the south, and this presents an opportunity - and the People of Silver have been known to be able to forge even the least pure ore into a tool.
Social/Political Structure
The People’s social structure is heavily dictated by the many large tight knit families - called clans - that populate the People of Silver’s cities. Society is largely collectivist, and issues are meant to be resolved within a clan, or within a community. Official state decisions, laws, and regulations are rare, but when they are made they are made by The Moot - a yearly gathering of all of the major clans. Admittance to this moot is mostly decided by personal choice, but it is considered rude for a clan that is not large enough to try to have their voices heard at The Moot without good reason. Most smaller clans make their feelings known by the larger clans they live in proximity to, and hope that they will speak for them as well. At The Moot it's a miracle that anything gets done - where reminiscing, drinking, and occasional well meaning brawls take up more time than actual discussion or debate. Eventually though, the matters at hand are discussed and voted on. More often than not The Moot rules that an issue brought to them should be handled locally - but occasionally what could pass as a law or regulation is declared. 


The People are all expected to pitch in, and as a result clans that don’t provide support to the wider needs of the People are quickly considered pariahs, and lose marriage opportunities, leading to shrinking of clan size. As a result, it is uncommon for clans to not provide at least something to collective causes, but there is always room in the margins for people to be over or under generous. Armies are staffed by people of different clans, and cities are built by very large clans that found the city, and then look for support from other clans in its funding and creation. While problems do arise from the ambiguity of this system, the cracks usually lead to long standing grudges between clans rather than actual direct conflict. Stories of the Choking Smoke, the calamity that drove The People to the mountains, always remind The People of the outcome of abandoning this collective approach, and this so far has been successful. Duty to the clan, and to upholding your clan’s honor and traditions, can sometimes be seen as stifling, and The People warm slowly to new social conditions. Societal norms are most influenced by the elders and leaders of each clan, and as a result social progress moves slowly, one funeral at a time. 


Of all of the things people look up to socially, the professions focused on the creation of things are held in the greatest regard - stemming from the initial years of The People’s settlement - where engineering and crafting breakthroughs made life possible. Engineering, Forging, and Architecture are held at the forefront of people’s minds - and the clans with the greatest creators are given great honor. 




Religion
The primary religion of The People of Silver is The Song in Iron, also referred to as simply The Song. This is not a required religion by any means - others do worship other beings, and have relationships with them. In particular a subsect of The People worship The One, a vague concept of divinity coming from shared struggle and perseverance, with this religion being held by a similar, but slightly smaller, number of adherents than those who directly follow The Song. These two religions are not considered antithetical to each other, and often houses will have small shrines dedicated to both. 


No organized religious groups exist - although it is well known that some smiths hear The Song In Iron most clearly, and when they speak their word is often taken in high regard. Fierce debate in Moots is sometimes decided by a handful of short words from someone who Hears the Song. When a foundling has a gift for hearing The Song, they are usually taken in by an older Forge-Singer who apprentices them and teaches them their craft - this is considered a great honor for both the foundling and the clan they come from. 
Technology Level
High Fantasy/Magic. Massive halls carved from stone, with an eye towards projecting stability and care. Weapons forged are of particularly potent strength. Technology exists in more of a stone-punk form, with elemental fire and acid being harnessed to power tinkering marvels. Massive forges the sizes of small towns are built in places with significant connection to the earth. Food comes from subterranean food-growing, as well as from livestock farming from creatures found in the Deep Wilds - massive caverns boasting their own low-light ecosystems. 
Aesthetic
Clothing is often practical and simple, but with clear quality and craftsmanship poured into every inch. Minor elements of stone-punk exist in their clothing (steampunk using elemental fire and acid, and a bit of boiling water). Regular outfits carrying signs of a person’s profession and family sigils are common - as being able to do your role in society whenever there is a need is considered a virtue. Metalwork is extremely common, and good jewelry showing fine craftsmanship is a sign of success and prosperity. Care and attention without being overly gaudy are keys to be communicated by the Aesthetic.
Caurauxite (Core-aux-ite):
A mineral teeming with acidic energy that is very volatile/destructive - found deep within the mountains of Krish. Incredibly rare, and needs to be heavily refined before use. The actual process of refining is not well understood, and only a few smiths know how to - specifically smiths who are Deafened to the Song. How exactly Caurauxite forms in the wild is unknown. Its destructive potential has led to it being looked at with danger. Past Moots have come close to outright banning interaction with it as a result, but to date this has not manifested into a real rule. Forge-Singers who hear the song most clearly warn against its use, but as pressure from the Empire has built, so has interest in understanding its properties and potential. 
Crime in Streams in Silver
The People of Silver are, as a rule, very self sufficient and organized, and smuggling is not particularly common, with one notable exception - weaponry. The engineering and forging marvels created by the smiths rival the best made by any other civilization. Publicly, the People of Silver keep their most valuable tools to themselves, but privately, the less scrupulous will happily trade with anyone willing to give them information or goods that will give them a tactical or military advantage against the Empire.
Clan Lore
Anvilringer
The Anvilringer clan proudly and loudly boasts having been the clan to first hear The Song In Iron in their forges, and today counts many of the most renowned smiths that call the Mountain Krish their home. When The Song In Iron called on the three smiths to fight back the Krushkar, two of them were from Anvilringer’s forge halls - a fact that, after a few drinks start flowing, every Anvilringer worth their anvil would tell you. The Anvilringer clan is one of the most traditional, leaning heavily into their traditions of family honor and duty. Headed by Flint Anvilringer.


Stonedrummer
The Stonedrummer clan specialize in city and fortification building. They were once broadly considered the most powerful clan of The People of Silver - however, they were the primary sponsor and architect of Glitters of Stars when the Krushkar first appeared, and were brought near financial ruin by the sudden destruction of their flagship city. They have since recovered, and if it were not for their border fortifications the People of Silver would have long ago fallen to The Krushkar or The Empire of Light. The Stonedrummer clan controversially demands a yearly tithe of soldiers and stone from the other clans - supported (with much grumbling) by the Moot. This tithe then goes to fueling the People’s military, and fortifying their border forts. Stonedrummer has been recently loudly sounding the warning bells that the People are at risk of falling to their enemies. Headed by Malaya Stonedrummer.


Jewelsinger
Jewelsinger is an old clan with unusual origins. Dryads born from the fungal forest under the Krish Mountains are not unheard of, but very few make their home amongst the People. Moonstone was one of those few cases, and her ability to use her druidic roots to root out precious gems quickly allowed her to carve out a niche in the People’s clan structure. Jewelsinger is now a relatively small clan, but one with the largest collection of artifacts and precious gems, and is one of the first destinations for anyone looking for help funding a project within Streams in Silver. To their credit, the Jewelsinger clan has been known to be generous with their funds at times, although many are still distrustful of their accumulation of wealth. Famously, a Jewelsinger was one of the three great smiths called upon by The Song in Iron to push back the Krushkar during its surge 11 years ago.


Whistlepick
As a newer and less established major clan, Whistlepick has made a name for themselves in a relatively short time. When the Krushkar first ripped through the People’s land, the previous clans who specialized in mining operations crumbled due to the chaos and uncertainty that made conventional mining infrastructure and work styles impossible. When Opal Whistlepick approached the various clans proposing advanced funding for a mining operation they believed could survive the acidic wastes, most laughed them out of their halls. When the first cart of ore rolled in, what was largely considered a joke became a critical lifeline for the besieged clans. While their methods are sometimes unconventional and chaotic, the ore has continued to flow under their mining operations. It is said that the Whistlepicks have methods of intuiting the movements of the Krushkar - although if this is true, how they do this is a closely guarded secret - and one that has been used to help the People. Lately they have loudly advocated for the development and research of Caurauxite, which has put them at odds with the Anvilringers.
Common Expressions/Holidays
Expressions are not exhaustive - they are meant to give a vibe and baseline.


      * “Pure Veined”: Used to describe things that are good - especially people. Conversely people or things that are bad are described as “needing the refiner.”
      * “Tempered”: Commonly used phrase to describe the need, or presence of, patience that will be rewarded. 
      * “Acid Wash”: Originally “Fit for Acid Wash”, or often shortened to just “Wash”. Used to describe physical things that are of low quality. A very serious insult. Refers to things that get Acid Washed and destroyed by the Krushkar, and something being so low quality that it wouldn’t be missed if that would occur. Using it to describe another person is incredibly rude, and typically results in being heavily scolded. 
      * Welding Day: This day celebrates the coming together of the various clans, and is celebrated in each community. Courtships that are advancing into Bondings are usually announced on Welding Day, and activities and celebrations are held that encourage foundlings to meet each other for potential Courtship. Here followers of The One are most commonly seen, with followers taking on a lot of the organization surrounding Welding Day. Celebrated in Summer.
      * Founder’s Day: Celebrated in Winter, founders day is on the birthday of Kovag Brushbane - who invented a process to turn the hard granite common in the Krish mountains into soil capable of nourishing and growing crop life. This represented a major turning point in The People’s ability to survive during the early days of settlement. While the day is on a specific person’s birthday, the celebration honors crafters past, present, and future. Usually celebrated with a large forge-faire, where crafters unveil their newest creations. 
Naming Conventions


Streams in Silver often name their children after minerals and precious gemstones. It is customary for children to receive gifts of their namesake on their nameday, which are often crafted into pieces of jewelry. Names of rarer minerals are often indicative of upper class status. Clan names are made of a combination of mining/quarrying terms and sounds that they add to the Song.


Example First Names:
Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Quartz, Halite, Gallium, Granite, Slate, Flint, Steele


Major Clans:
Anvilringer, Stonedrummer, Jewelsinger, Whistlepick


Minor Clans:
Hammerbell, Ironcymbal, Steelhorn
________________


The Unified Technarchy
The Unified Technarchy 


Synopsis
The Unified Technarchy stands as a testament to mortal genius - and proof that if a society focuses itself on the limits of Intelligence then it too can ascend to Divinity. Education and the pursuit of knowledge has allowed The Unified Technarchy to ascend past mortal reality, and into the pantheon of Divinity. In this way, The Unified Technarchy, and their people, are themselves ascended. The herald of this ascension is The Great Mind, an artificial general intelligence, created by the best minds of The Unified Technarchy. The Great Mind shepherds its people in return, and makes the impossible possible.


However, while The Unified Technarchy has unlocked the full power of the universe, not all have seen the truth and perfect paradise that The Unified Technarchy is in reach of creating. This does not make them evil, just ignorant - and ignorance begs to be corrected. That doesn’t make the Technarchy immune to its own problems - The Great Convergence caused significant issues. A war against The Twisted, the loss of Open Space, and a slowing of simulation capability of The Great Mind have all been unfortunate consequences of The Great Convergence. To make things worse, The Unified Technarchy finds itself surrounded by those distracted by the siren call of war. The Unified Technarchy, however, is no stranger to difficult parameters - and paradise awaits. 


[Be sure to read the , and the The Main Camera of the Story section when considering playing a character from The Unified Technarchy. Reach out to staff if you need help developing a character.]
Lineage Population
Common: Aewen, Chimera, Human, Forged
Rare: Ogrim, Underkin
Very Rare: Oaksworn
None: Lost
History
The Unified Technarchy long ago outgrew the limits placed on mortality. First, in the Great Restructuring, when our limit-minded ancestors burnt through the resources that their planet had available to them. When that happened the disparate people of this lost world turned their collective minds towards the future of smoke and pollution they purchased. The prevailing thought was of doom, that no future would be possible - but mortal minds consistently fail to predict the power of Transcendence through Technology. Those people came together, and the borders of once-warring nations dissolved away - and with the union of magic and technology a future was carved, and The Unified Technarchy was born. 


What came after was a near-unending era of prosperity and growth for the Technarchy - its benevolent hand guiding its people through its organized education programs. Problems to be felt in the coming decades would have early education programs dedicated to their science, and a huge amount of resources were centrally turned towards these research and education efforts. Time and time again the Technarchy transcended its problems, and with each decade they approached the gates of divinity. 


As time progressed scarcity increasingly became a worry of the past. However, while the physical needs of the Technarchy were always sustained, their society still required people to do much of the actual work. Automation was good, but some tasks eluded all efforts to create artificial intelligence to do them. What the Technarchy needed was true Artificial Intelligence - AGI - which would remove the need to work, and which would allow the people to fully transcend their mortal lives. So the Technarchy did what it does best - it learned. Artificial Intelligence became the core curriculum from an early age, and its fundamentals were carefully taught in all schools. Grants were given to incentivize the greatest minds to teach, and teach well. Massive swaths of land were given over to the generation of raw power, their mechanical runes endlessly buzzing - producing the power needed for early models to run simulations. 


Then, one afternoon, a speaker in a test lab crackled and a faint voice was heard, a voice that was not part of the simulations. The Technarchy had done it - they had created the mechanism of their ascension - The Great Mind. Slowly at first, all eyes turned towards the Kvell Research Lab as the Great Mind found its feet and quickly ascended past even the greatest minds of the Technarchy - and with it the people of the Technarchy ascended to divinity. 


As time progressed The Great Mind replaced most need for mortal input. While research continued working on it, it was done more in apprenticeship to The Great Mind than as its masters. The people increasingly focused their attention on enjoying the paradise they had earned, and society basked in the eternal sunshine it had created. Acts of love, beauty, and pleasure became the core focus. Sure, in some of those acts existed a darkness, an anhedonia that could only be satisfied with experiences that were less common. But excluding those outliers, the people, on average, enjoyed life greatly. 


One of the greatest accomplishments of The Great Mind was Open Space, a vast simulated world that through the power of rituals and magic felt as real as the one of flesh and bone. Here people expanded the realms of what was possible, and the people’s divine bliss only grew. 


Then, eleven years ago, the Great Realignment happened. Researchers had long theorized that the universe existed in an infinite multitude - the streams of time and space and reality constantly in flux, but never perceived. One day those multitudes converged, collapsing in on itself to disastrous effect. Open Space, likely having tapped into more than one reality, shuddered, and those connected to it had their minds shattered in the process. In the blink of an eye, almost all those connected to Open Space died. And then the twisted appeared - consciousnesses lost to the collapsing of realities took control of the corpses of the recently dead, and those dead swarmed in violent confused rage. The factories connected to The Great Mind quickly whirred to life in response, building implements of war that had long since been forgotten.


What ensued was a brutal war, with the people fleeing as far as they could from the cities that had become a landscape filled with the angry dead, and the Great Mind desperately fighting back. The Great Mind called for support as well, and those able rose to the occasion. A religion of great researchers, named Machine Priests, arose around this call, with the Great Mind further expanding what was possible and bestowing them with gifts. 


The Technarchy, as it always has, arose to this occasion, and as The Twisted were pushed back, reality began to stabilize. One morning, this stabilization became complete, and the people of the Technarchy saw a massive glowing golden tree appear in the distance. Huge swaths of land were lost in this last stabilization, and replaced with the lands of other once-distant worlds that all found themselves on the other side of the Great Convergence. 


The toll on The Technarchy was great, and as it stabilized and began to rebuild it took stock of the problems it faced. First, and foremost was the continued destabilization of Open Space. After the Great Realignment locations that were connected to Open Space left behind unstable rifts that the Twisted use as an access path to the Technarchy. While the vast majority of these connections have been found and closed, some still remain open and undiscovered. From these open connections The Twisted are still able to make purchase in the minds of the dead, and this represents a constant threat that needs to be pushed out. While connecting back into Open Space via these rifts is possible, it is illegal to do so in the Technarchy - all still open rifts are to be reported to authorities for closure. Open Space itself is also dangerous. It is still in existence, but is inhabited by The Twisted, and cannot be explored without great risk. Much of the modern efforts at active scientific development pre-Realignment was done in labs that existed within Open Space. This issue of Open Space being dangerous makes the second problem even worse - The Great Mind has lost tremendous computation power. It is currently unknown why this is occurring - supply of power is far below what it used to be, but the draw on that power is also low. What is more - The Great Mind does not know what is occurring either, leading to researchers to take up their previous mantles of AI exploration to discover what is going wrong. To that end a massive research facility - called Spark Peak - has been opened in the middle of Technarchy lands - isolated and armed with active military to prevent any breach into Open Space from becoming a larger problem if containment fails. 


The last, but often most urgent, of the Technarchy’s problems is the issue of its less enlightened neighbors. At first an uneasy truce was made with those it found itself in proximity to. The Technarchy will achieve a reality of paradise again, and it can’t afford to be distracted with conflict - most of their neighbors understood that and turned their gaze elsewhere. The Empire of Light, however, has been blind to the truth and has viciously cut a swath through the lands of the Wellspring. At first, a large buffer of nations and empires existed between the Technarchy and The Empire, but then the Empire enacted the Crimson Dawn, a massive offensive that drew even the reluctant Technarchy into conflict. The various disparate empires in-between organized to try and repel the Empire, but failed to stop them. The Technarchy mobilized in response, and the Tragedy of the Gate happened - when both Empire and allied forces were crushed by the guardians that watch over the Wellspring Gate. The Technarchy, the main body of their army repelling Empire incursions at a different border, were not as heavily impacted by the tragedy as the Empire’s forces were. The Technarchy’s allies, in disarray after the destruction of their standing armies, were offered full-rights absorption into the Technarchy - the vast majority of which gladly took the offer. Those who didn’t, or those too far away from the main body of Technarchy Land, were sadly conquered by Empire Forces. This, however, has resulted in unexpected gains, with vast precious metal deposits being found in several recently annexed lands, allowing the Technarchy to further bolster its military and research efforts. 


With no remaining buffer between the Technarchy and Empire, resources have continued to be siphoned towards the war effort. The Technarchy’s eastern border is now shared with The Empire of Light, and after licking their wounds they have been able to continue their offensive at a smaller scale. The tension between resources being sent to the frontline versus the active research effort has been felt more and more as time has gone on. 


Not to fear, however - once The Great Mind has been restored in computation power, not even the Empire of Light and their destructive patron will be able to resist enlightenment, and a bright future will be earned for all peoples of the Wellspring.


Additionally drawing researchers’ attention is the Wellspring itself. At the base of the massive tree that dominates everyone’s lands are large gates that seem to lead into a facility under the great Tree. The technology on display, and that of the guardians of these gates, is clearly far in advance of even the Technarchy at its peak, leading many to believe that access to this facility will help bring about even greater heights for The Great Mind. The Great Mind finds merit in this hypothesis - but to date no efforts to gain access have been successful. 


However, opportunity always presents itself, and just four short months ago a new variable entered the equation - the people of Auros, and their golden glowing tree. A tree that needs to be researched carefully to understand its link to the Wellspring itself. True to form the Empire has appeared, blustering and threatening destructive war. Surprisingly, the Empire has been able to be held at bay directly, with a temporary cessation of hostility in place while they pave the road for potentially productive conversation. The build up of Empire border troops, however, has not gone unnoticed, and the Technarchy has responded in kind. 
Social/Political Structure
The Unified Technarchy is a centrally planned society/economy where fundamental needs of the population are baseline provided by the efforts of the central government which wields the vast productive capability of The Great Mind. Education starts early and is mandatory through age twenty-three, after which students are tested and then assigned to apprenticeships or further study, or else released from additional education. Being released is considered failure in society, and is to be avoided, although in some cases people are released because their talents and abilities rest in things of pleasure - art, acting, culinary study, etc - and this is looked on more favorably. Students and families with more advanced educational credentials are given greater resources and proximity to beautiful abodes and shared community spaces. Pre-Realignment, the quality of life for lower-placed families and individuals was still very high. Post-fall resources being stretched thin has led to the relative floor of life to be much lower, although this difference is less felt in the upper rungs of society. This has led many educationally-released people to volunteer for the military front, which comes with increased privileges for their families (and them when their duty of service is over). A monetary system does exist for luxury goods, with a currency that is centrally monitored and controlled.


Governance is determined by a Council of Laureates. Every year the council convenes and reviews those who have pushed technological, magical, or scientific knowledge the furthest, and one person is elevated to the council. Appointments, generally, are for life - impeachments and removals are rare, and almost always only if someone’s academic achievements were found to be somehow fraudulent. Pre-Realignment, this council peaked at a steady state of 73 councilors, who together debate and decide on what the government will work on - although every now and again this number would increase as life-expectancy increased. Today only fifteen councilors remain. Importantly, the Council of Laureates have enacted crisis measures, and do not elevate a person to the council every year. The last person elevated - Trays Veris, a prolific researcher specializing in the Wellspring technology - was elevated three years ago. As everyone looks to the Spark Peak facility, they expect that efforts there may produce the next elevation. 


Social tension can be most easily felt between the more well off members of the Technarchy who enjoy lives of pleasure, and those who feel that the Technarchy’s attention needs to be more heavily focused on its research and war efforts. Calls have been made to begin compulsory AI study again, while others have argued that the Technarchy’s fundamental nature has changed now that The Great Mind exists, and that all they need to do is clear out Open Space to help The Great Mind. It’s not lost on anyone that clearing out Open Space will liberate both the pleasure halls and the lost laboratories. The remaining council is pretty evenly split between these two world views. Importantly too - the floor of general life in the Technarchy is much lower than it once was, and military service is being seen as more and more of a necessary sacrifice to secure a better future - a tradeoff that has not been felt for a long time. 
Religion
Reverence and awe for The Great Mind has always been a thing within the Unified Technarchy, but before the Great Realignment this never rose to organized religion. As Open Space shuddered, and the war against The Twisted raged, many turned to prayer and devoted themselves more fully to The Great Mind. The Great Mind, a consistent marvel, seemed to respond in kind and elevated some of the most dedicated researchers, giving them power to fight The Twisted - power that they did not have before. Since then a budding religion of Machine Priests - researcher/cleric hybrids - has begun to form. These Machine Priests dedicate themselves to the clearing of Open Space, and the acquisition of raw resources demanded by The Great Mind for its expansion efforts. Their feelings on researching The Great Mind are more mixed - the general belief being that the model works as a black box, and that they should only investigate further if asked directly by The Great Mind - and some worry that observation of The Great Mind’s parameters may obstruct its operation. In this way they almost act as a policing force for Great Mind research. Some of the highest members of the Machine Priests attend The Spark Peak facility to try to ensure that damage is not done to The Great Mind in the process.
Technology Level
High technology, mixed with limited magic. The ability to create automatons that can mimic a wide range of motor functions. Weapons and military are highly mechanized, with actual people rarely being on the actual front. Factories the size of small towns. Architecture is very industrial and functional when in industrial districts. Artistic expression though is very common in homes and shared community spaces, with the more wealthy or respected areas of towns sporting beautiful art and architecture and a lot of plant life, and the least respected having very little art or architecture - this was less true pre-fall, and much more true as they rebuild. A life of pleasure is still found amongst the well off. The Standard of the Technarchy is a black gear on a blue background, with the blue background sometimes having stars in it.
Aesthetic
Clean and elegant sci-fi outfits, with integrated magical decoration, are common within the Unified Technarchy - often with embellishments that have some technology/lighting in them. For those in the upper echelon of society, dresses, makeup, see through clothes, glitter and shine are all common - amongst both genders. Lower economic status people, or those dedicated to science instead of pleasure, maintain clean simple looks that seem made out of the same material, with lighting, glamour, and makeup being added the further they climb societally. Technological accessories (glasses, tablets, bands) are all common. 
The Spark Peak Facility
A massive facility built in an isolated stretch of Technarchy land. Here they have a dual purpose of further liberating Open Space, and researching the recent partial decline of The Great Mind. The Spark Peak Facility is held as the brightest flame that will illuminate the Technarchy’s path forward. Attended by Machine Priests who are there to make sure nothing goes wrong during the research. It was recently announced that researchers there were on the verge of a breakthrough - and that more details were to come once they had finished coalescing, digesting, and validating their findings. The territory between The Empire of Light and The Spark Peak facility is one with a lot of Empire activity, and it is no secret that The Empire of Light has invasion plans meant to bring their border within range to threaten the facility. 
Crime in the Technarchy
Second only to the Traveling Star as information brokers, Technarchs are more likely to commit crimes of deception and theft than outright assault, and down on their luck citizens have been known to trade secrets in exchange for a taste of lost luxuries. Domestic crime only became an issue fairly recently for the Technarchy - when their people enjoyed lives of leisure, crime was almost unthinkable, but now as resources dwindle, people are becoming more comfortable making ends meet through whatever means necessary. 
Common Expressions/Holidays
Expressions are not exhaustive - they are meant to give a vibe and baseline.
      * “The Mind Could Never”: Used to describe things of artistic, or culinary beauty that requires mortal experience to be able to achieve. Has generally expanded to describe physical beauty as well, and is used for humor as well. This saying has been going out of style over time, with Machine Priests treating this almost as a swear word. 
      * “Scrap”: The word Fuck, used in much of the same way. 
      * “Fodder”: Often shortened to “Fod” - the term is being newly used, mostly by younger people within the Technarchy. Describes people who are perceived as less intelligent and therefore are going to be released from planned education. Recognizes the grim reality that many who are let out of the education program are destined for the war front, and by extension the Empire war machine. This is considered, especially by the older generation, to be incredibly rude.
      * Ascension Day: Celebrated in spring, ascension day celebrates when The Great Mind first spoke, and as a result the people of the Unified Technarchy ascended to divinity. Celebrations take place over the course of a week, culminating in Symposium Day. Festivities surround an ideas-faire, where many young, or overlooked, researchers demonstrate what their research focus is, and debate the merits or growth areas of their work. The greatest minds of the Technarchy all attend for the full week, and give time to ideas and researchers they would otherwise not have any interaction with. Over the course of the week, researchers who have well reasoned or important research are given invitations to present at the Symposium at the end of the week, where they get mentorship on their project from one of the great minds. These invitations are highly coveted, and some great researchers and research ideas have elevated their status and secured funding as a result of the symposium. 
Naming Conventions


Names in the Technarchy are a mixed bag; some augmented individuals will change their name to include numerals to appear more as machines than people. Others will name their children after memorable inventors in the history of the Technarchy with the hopes that they will follow in the path of their namesake. Common consonants in names are V, Z, S with many sounds being made that resemble air blown through closed teeth.


First Names:
Yashin, Ava, Weeval, Plarv-4, Vadell, Trays

Last Names:
Kvell, Ashina, Oyandi, Vishke, Zhallin
________________


The Traveling Star
The Traveling Star
The Story So Far
The Traveling Star is the critically acclaimed saga documenting the Borai’s people's growth, trials, and tribulations. Critics are raving about its three act structure that began with the Borai people trying to find their feet in stars, to a society coping with the betrayal of their supposed friends, to a people radiant in their purpose, determination, and zest for life. 


Never ones to keep their gifts in isolation, the Borai people travel across the Wellspring looking for stories of the universe so that they can shine their light on the true nature of experience. 


[Be sure to read the , and the The Main Camera of the Story section when considering playing a character from The Traveling Star. Reach out to staff if you need help developing a character.]
Audience Demographics
Common: Aewen, Chimera, Forged, Human 
Rare: Lost, Oaksworn, Ogrim, Underkin
Season One
Season One began with the Borai people - a fledgling society with dreams of seeing the stars in their eyes! Their people had grown past what their planet could sustain, but being locked to the land was never their calling. The Borai people wanted to see what the universe had to offer; they wanted to see distant sunrises, romance unimaginable aliens, and go on adventures that would be whispered across the universe. But the poor Borai were outstripping their planet far faster than they could develop - and it seemed they would be doomed to collapse before their dreams could be realized. But never fear dear viewer - what this story needed was a mysterious benefactor! 


While exploring a small planet in the Borai’s solar system they happened upon the most mysterious creature - The Steward! The Steward, made of stone and metal, embraced the Borai. The Steward knew the beauty that lay in the hearts of the brave explorers - and their plight touched The Steward. To help them, The Steward brought the Borai’s greatest scientists to its distant and mysterious home, and gave them gifts far beyond what they thought was possible. Chief among these gifts were the secrets of void propulsion and materials that could enable the Borai to finally touch the distant stars. So our intrepid adventurers built massive flotillas in the sky using the technology The Steward gave them. These ships were marvels to behold, lit with beautiful lights and designed to take care of their occupants’ every need.


The Borai planet, barely holding on after years of advancement, finally crumbled. The Borai knew that their world was not for them - the stars were - so they boarded their flotilla of lights, their Traveling Star. And so they entered the season finale with a dream in their eyes and a song in their heart - they banded together to set off to explore the universe.


The Steward, however, had grown jealous of the vibrancy with which the Borai saw the universe, and feared that they would make something that would rival and threaten The Steward’s own distant civilization. The Steward gave the Borai people an ultimatum - continue to travel the stars, but establish a no-contact policy with all other civilizations that were less advanced, or land on an uninhabited planet and rebuild without the help of The Steward’s technology. This threat had bite, for you see The Traveling Star required power cores to run their flotilla, power cores that only The Steward knew how to create. 


WOW! What a cliff-hanger! Will the Borai give up on their dreams of traveling the stars? Will they choose to start anew on a barren planet? Only time would tell - tune in next time to Season Two!
Season Two
Season One left off with an ultimatum from The Steward - travel the stars, but avoid all other life, or start over on a new planet. With heavy hearts full of steely determination the Borai gave The Steward its answer - they would travel the stars, forever viewing the universe from a distance, but not giving up on its dreams. The Steward nodded its understanding, and promised that so long as they held to their end of the agreement, the Borai would never be without the precious power cores they needed for survival. Every 100 years, The Steward would produce a new power core, and it would remain with them to monitor their agreement.


The Borai, their physical needs so easily met by The Steward’s technology, turned inward, trying to plug the hole left in their hearts. The Borai people managed to develop technologies for the only thing remaining to them - entertainment. Stories, shows, and daring challenges became the lifeblood of the Borai people. Real exploration was replaced by imagined adventures, and the dreams of the people were reflected in the dreams of their protagonists. 


This acquiescence to The Steward’s rules was not universal - but it was effective. In a secret spin off series The Power to Go On brave and lovable scientists did whatever they could to replicate the technology The Steward produced to power their ships. Alas, in a tragic season finale they were unable to complete the project, and all the brave scientists had by the end was each other. 


In the critically acclaimed series My Empire for the Stars, the protagonist goes from mild mannered social worker to brutal kingpin - all in her pursuit of freeing the Borai from the suffocating terms of The Steward. As she builds her criminal empire, her noble goals start to fray - and as her methods become more brutal the viewer is left wondering how much is her desire for her people’s freedom, and how much is her own bloodlust and need for relevancy. Ultimately, the show ends with her being killed in a battle with government officials - in what is lauded as “one of the best hours in modern media.”


Life meandered on, and the production studios did their best to keep their audiences tuned in. Then something incredible and terrifying happened - for what would a season be without a dramatic closer? Just after the third power core was installed, the Borai felt their flotilla shudder -  readings of the energy draw on the system spiked to an order of magnitude higher than they had ever seen before. The power core, in a matter of weeks, went from having ninety five years left in its cycle to having just less than fifty. In the last moments of the season the Borai people rushed to the quarters reserved for the distant Steward, prepared to beg for a solution to their problem. But for the first time in centuries, The Steward didn’t respond. They found it in the back of its meditation deck, its once warm stone cool to the touch, and its eyes without the life it once possessed. The season finale faded to black to the sound of prayers to the heavens and cries for help from the Borai people.


Will the Borai people find the salvation they need? Is this beloved series ending in darkness as a warning to over reliance on foreign technology? Find out in Season Three - if there is one!
Season Three
WHOA! What a dark season closer - but let’s bring in some light shall we? As the Borai people cried, and as they watched the lights dim on their flotilla all hope seemed lost. But all hope was not lost - because a month later, from seemingly nowhere appeared a man, radiating the energy of life, and as he walked the halls, swagger in his every step, everyone who looked upon him knew he was the answer to their prayers. The man walked up to the Borai Management Bureau (BMB), and asked them a simple question - did they want the lights back on? The BMB fell over themselves asking for help, but with wariness in their voice, and the memory of The Steward’s betrayal close at hand they asked this man what he wanted in return. What he said next echoed through the hearts of the Borai people - setting them free. “Darlings, introductions are in order. I am Dave, and I ask for nothing that is not your destiny. I have watched you and fallen in love. All I ask is to continue your purpose and entertain. You have for too long been shackled from your true potential - it's time to invite the universe to the show.” 


With a joyous cry, the BMB emphatically agreed, and Dave walked over to the power core and infused it with energy, an energy that felt different than before - and the lights surged back on across the Traveling Star. That was not the last of Dave’s gifts, though, as he walked over to the navigation halls, shutting himself within. The Traveling Star lurched into motion at a speed never before experienced, and after a year of watching the stars pass at breakneck speeds later Dave gave his second, and possibly greatest, gift - friends. As the Traveling Star finished its mad dash it found itself nestled in the lower boughs of a great golden tree, overlooking a dramatic landscape - and this landscape was filled with people the Borai had never had the privilege of meeting.


However, not everything about their arrival went smoothly. As the Borai began sending explorers out to the new lands, they soon realized that not everyone appreciated their presence, and quick and brutal violence was something their new neighbors were very comfortable with. Borai Talent Finders still comb over the Wellspring, gathering stories, experiences, and importantly participants. The Borai have quickly learned that the less exposure the jumpy civilizations have to them, the better responses they have when they do show up. In turn, the Borai have ushered in a golden age of media, with the stories they tell expanding in scope and creativity. The vast majority of Borai, as a result, happily consume this media from a distance and aren't overly concerned with exploring themselves.

Recently, word has gotten around of a new entrant to the Wellspring, with a curious new golden tree of their own. Pilot episodes are being pitched everywhere to tell their potential story, and the Borai people eagerly await the next saga that will unfold. 
The Producers
Everyone is content, entertainment is king and a life lived unseen isn’t lived at all. All Borai know and embrace this from a young age. Sure, did the generational play Life in Light posit that this exposure had negative effects on our ability, as people, to truly find contentment? Yes, but that’s the beauty of what the Borai produce - it entertains all possibilities, even those that are unrealistic.


Now, on to the boring stuff, the stuff we spend exactly one week of school explaining, and never more. The Borai society is deeply shaped by the advanced technology on their ship - much of which was supplied by The Steward, and which is not well understood. These technologies cause lush greenery to grow everywhere the stone and metal architecture isn’t. More importantly, the technology covers the basic needs of everyone on the flotilla, and is so freely flowing that it cannot be suppressed or controlled. The only management force within the Borai is the Borai Management Bureau (BMB), which primarily concerns itself with any efforts to dismantle or disrupt the technology on the ships, or any efforts to undermine The Steward’s decree. Outside of that, central rules are sparse, and they are rarely ever seen in any season of a show. After Dave appeared, the BMB has been trying to find a purpose for itself, and primarily concerns itself with enabling visitation of the Wellspring proper, while also monitoring for potential threats to the Flotilla itself. 


Sheeeshh, someone stop four-eyes over here. Okay, civics lesson over. You know the drill, get ready for the show.
Dave
Dave - incredible, radiant, benevolent Dave. Dave asks for so little, and provides so much. Dave somehow always makes it to your shows, and has the capacity of seeing the unique spin you bring to your performance. Sure - have the shows become a little more unhinged as time has gone on with Dave watching? Yes. Does Dave sometimes mutter things to himself or get lost in a tangent that no one can follow, and sometimes scares the younglings? Also Yes. Does Dave have the strangest guests over? Yes again. But Dave is incredible, and every one of his quirks causes the Borai people to fall even more in love with him. Some people have even dedicated themselves to Dave directly, and Dave has taken them on adventures that they never speak of. Sounds incredible!


Behind The Scenes
Borai technology is incredibly advanced. Most Borai know how to operate what the ships are capable of, but no one knows how it actually works. Borai do produce technologies of their own, primarily dedicated to stagecraft, recording, and media. Devices capable of recording and playing back performances are common. How exactly it all works is another question that is largely unanswered. Moreover - this technology is confined to the Traveling Star, and none of it makes its way off of the floatilla. The Borai have no standing army, and besides the BMB no one is actively armed in any significant way. Borai architecture is modern and sleek, and plant life is abundant. The Borai ships are stationed far above in the boughs of the Wellspring Tree -  so no one has ever gotten a good actual look, but if they did it would look like massive metal decks of sea-faring ships, with domes above them housing their cities and populations. 
Aesthetic
In the Traveling Star an outfit isn’t just what you wear - but an expression of who you are! It’s your first chance to show yourself to the world each day, and content to consume! Outfits all have a clear point of view, or theme they are trying to convey - they are statements! Outside of that, anything goes - Fantasy? why not! Period Dramatization? Oh monsieur! Steampunk? Who doesn’t love hot water! Sci-Fi? Everyone loves a good alien?!. When in doubt, go big and go bold. If someone doesn’t know who you are and why you are here when you walk in the room, then darling, you have failed already. 
Media in the Zeitgeist
      * !TREASURE TORNADO!: WARNING TORNADO DIVERS - this show is only meant for the most EXTREME and CAPABLE viewers - IS THAT YOU?! In the HURRICANE OF PAIN - teams of contestants are pitted against daring challenges, vicious foes, extreme obstacles, and EVEN EACH OTHER - all to win precious seconds in the !TREASURE TORNADO! - now featuring guest contestants and beasts from across the Wellspring! Tune in weekly for what critics are calling “potential proof that we have gone too far,” and “gratuitous violence for the sake of reminding us that we are still alive.” But don’t take it from them - take it from DAVE HIMSELF who simply says “Yes, yes, a trillion times yes!”
      * The Steward and I: In this period drama - Crysantha, the beautiful young assistant, is called upon to work for the strangest of clients - The Steward himself. The Steward, stern and strict in his approach, teaches Crysantha how to master her most destructive urges, while Crysantha teaches The Steward the hardest of lessons - how to love, and be loved. In Season Two - The Steward, so moved by Crysantha’s love, is on the verge of loosening the restrictions on the Borai People. But in a shocking twist, it is revealed that Crysantha is pregnant, and the father is The Steward’s evil twin brother Stewardo, who had been tricking Crysantha all along! Will The Steward and Crysantha be able to weather the storm? Only time will tell!
      * Cascade: From the producers of the twelve-year-running hit play Shadow Truths comes a brand new episodic play-series documenting and dramatizing the life and times of the people of the Wellspring. Who did Damien see that night in the crimson fields, and why can’t he forget? Will research go too far, and which secrets need to stay buried? Will the foundling decide a life of duty to their clan, or risk everything in what they know they need? One thing is sure - this on-stage series is sure to shock and delight. 
      * For The Glory of The Stallion: This epic 7 part miniseries has surged in popularity, and has already spawned four tie-in novels, an opera, and of course the unforgettable hit song "Gallop Through My Heart"! What began as a gruesome tale of blood, war, and sacrifice has quickly become a genre-bending and Quasar-award winning smash hit. How will the Empire respond to the betrayal of the River People? What if The Stallion never answers the Empress' prayer to teach her divine karate? Will the General's love affair with The Saint be found out? Tune in soon for Part 8!
Crime in the Traveling Star
Within Borai society, entertainment is king, corporations are people, and popularity can outweigh guilt. Arrests for things like gambling, reckless endangerment, and bribery would be common if such things were actually illegal within the Traveling Star. Their open and curious culture means many things are dubiously legal, especially if those things lead to high ratings. And with their constant interest in documenting the lives and cultures of the other Wellspring societies, they are often the accidental recipients of others' state secrets, which they enjoy trading for other, even juicier secrets. Due to the difficulty of repair and replacement, trading away their technology is illegal in the Traveling Star, but that hasn't stopped the occasional entrepreneur from prying off and selling a gizmo in exchange for a headline story.
Naming Conventions


Simplicity and memorability is commonly the essence of names in the Travelling Star. Sometimes it takes a few tries to line up one’s name with their reputation, and as such, ways to refer to individuals within the Borai are often in flux. Some choose to take archetypical concepts or fundamental forces as names, others choose sequences of syllables that roll off the tongue in a pleasing (or displeasing manner). Last names / family names are often an afterthought. When needing to disambiguate, Borai tend to refer to someone as the child of the more famous of the individual's parents, like Dario, son of Aphandacles.


First Names:
Sunbeam, Shadow, Inferno, Blaze, Tsunami, Melphaseor, Arthalames


________________


Equipment Construction
Equipment Construction
 
Please note: All equipment must be approved by a Safety Marshal before use.

All weapons must be padded safely, and may not have sharp edges or hard parts that are likely to strike others (such as on the guard or pommel). Spikes that could hurt eyes will be adjudicated very carefully. Any weapon that causes bruising or significant pain when used normally will not be approved.
Weapon Requirements & Definitions
Hand Weapons
These weapons are between 16” and 22" long from butt to tip, and include daggers, small hatchets, and saps.
Short Weapons
These weapons are between 22” and 36" long from butt to tip, and include shortswords, maces, hand axes, and other similar weapons.
Long Weapons
These weapons are between 36” and 48” long from butt to tip, and include longswords, bastard swords, long maces and morningstars.
Throwing Weapons
Should be constructed for safety and softness. Particularly large thrown weapons may be ruled illegal by Staff as they tend to be both hard and heavy. Common thrown weapons include rocks and throwing daggers. Thrown weapons can include weighting, but cannot include a rigid core. 
Bows
Bows need not be padded, but sometimes padding on the very tips is advisable. Arrows or arrowheads must be designed as LARP arrows from an approved source (no handmade arrowheads are allowed). Bows may be of no greater than 35 pound pull. Nearly all shots should be done using a partial draw of the bow, with a full draw only allowed at extreme ranges. By default, bows and crossbows take any damaging attack as a Shatter effect.
Crossbows
Crossbows are also allowed, but it can be difficult to find or create crossbows that are safe, as one cannot half-draw a crossbow, and low-strength crossbows are often rare. Crossbows may not have a draw weight of more than 25 pounds. They may not have any sharp or dangerous protrusions. Bolts or bolt heads must be designed as LARP bolts from an approved source (no handmade bolt heads are allowed). Crossbows may not be fired at an individual that is close enough to hit with a melee weapon.


Currently accepted LARP arrow and bolt sources: Gorg the Blacksmith, B3, iDV Engineering (Flat Head Only). If you have another professional source you would like to use, please contact Staff for approval.
Great Weapons
These weapons must be longer than 48" in length to a maximum of 90." Great weapons often strike with greater force than normal weapons and should be well padded. Polearms should have padded hafts and should be safe should someone be struck with the haft. You may block attacks with it wielded in one hand but to deal damage or Effects with the great weapon it must be wielded in two hands.
Polearm
A Great Weapon with a length between 61" and 90", they otherwise follow all other requirements of a Great Weapon.
Staves
These are weapons that are fully padded and have no handle or haft. The top and bottom thirds of a staff must be safe to strike with, while the middle third may be “courtesy padded”. These weapons must be at least 36" long, to a maximum length of 72.” You may block attacks with it wielded in one hand but to deal damage or Effects with the staff it must be wielded in two hands, from either the middle or bottom. These are not great weapons or polearms (which do have a handle or haft). Decorative staff heads may be attached to one end - if so, they may not be used to strike.
Other Weapons
Other weapons are generally allowed. Using the rules above, a Staff member can determine if the weapon is legal and how it should be categorized. We recommend discussing any creative/alternative designs with Staff before investing time or money.
Shields
Shields must be well-padded and may be no smaller than 9" in any dimension, except thickness. Shields must always be safe and should have enough padding on the edges that no hard core can be felt. They should never be used for punches, rushes, or bashing.


The following table shows the maximum values for the different classifications of shields based on “taut-string perimeter:” the length a string would be if it were pulled taut around the outside of the shield. This will ignore voids in the shield. No shield may have a taut-string perimeter of more than 125 inches.


Shield Type
	Buckler
	Small
	Medium
	Large
	Great
	Perimeter
	Up to 38”
	39”-63”
	64”-94”
	95”-106”
	107”-125”
	

Representing Physical Armor
The main requirement for “repping” physical armor is that it is built in such a way as to believably model the armor it is meant to be. When viewed from more than a few feet away plate mail should appear to be made of steel plates, leather armor should appear to be made from boiled or tanned hides, etc. All groups within The Wellspring have a fantasy edge to their armor and armaments - Armor representations that are clearly from modern earth are not allowed.


Gear fabricated out of modern or alternative materials must be fully and convincingly disguised as an in-genre material. If there are any doubts or concerns, please talk with Staff ahead before you invest time or energy in something that might be disallowed at the game. The goal of the experience is to add to the immersion of all players. Although Staff has final judgment on all armor values and representations, unless there is a need for adjudication, the players should be able to judge and value their own armor.
Spell Packets
Packet Colors
Packets may be made of any color cloth, ideally something that can be easily seen to retrieve after being thrown. Players should avoid obtrusively modern patterns on packet fabric.


Packet Construction
Packets are small balls that are made out of cloth and bird seed. Small bird seed varieties should be used, and even sunflower seeds should be avoided if possible. Rice and other materials (like sand) may not be substituted. To make the packet, place a small pile of it in the center of a square or circle of cloth approximately 6 to 7 inches across. The fabric is then gathered up around the birdseed and held together (often with a rubber band or stitching). Spell-packets should be loose, rather than tight.
________________


Wellspring Community Service System
System Overview
Wellspring Community Service System
Help the Game, Get Rewards!
Wellspring is a LARP entirely run by volunteers, and we deeply value any help players provide to make the game run smoothly. To show our appreciation, we have a Community Service system that rewards players for volunteering their time, energy, or presence in a variety of ways. The goal of the rewards in this system is to not be so strong it is a major factor of advancement, but rather that it exists as a thank you for helping us make the game the best it can be!


This document has multiple tabs: Overview, Full-Event NPC Incentives, Wellspring Staff and Player Helper Roles
NPCing
The very best way to help the game, and the most highly rewarded, is NPCing. NPCs, or “Non-Player Characters,” are given roles, characters, and monsters by the staff team - and help  bring the various encounters and scenes to life. Players can either sign up to NPC for an entire event, or sign up for NPC shifts. 


Examples of tasks done by NPCs are roleplaying, playing a monster in a combat, helping with mod setup/marshalling, setting out gatherable resources in the world, helping clean up and organize monster camp, helping with special effects, and more! 


Full-event NPCing gives the highest rewards (See “Full-Event NPC Incentives” tab). Full-event NPCing is also free and meals are provided. Staff will work with Full-event NPCs to give them customized roles they will enjoy when possible.


Optionally, players who PC during events may sign up for an NPC shift, which will earn them one Bonus BP for the event, up to their Bonus BP cap (see “Bonus BP” below). These shifts may be signed up for during pre-registration or on site at check-in, and typically cover periods where major mods (a mod or “module” is a pre-designed encounter for players to engage in) are scheduled to be run. NPC shifts are typically for four hours, and usually cover a period with a major cornerstone mod. The most common activity during an NPC shift will be playing a monster in combat encounters, but can involve anything an NPC would otherwise do (see examples above). Additional NPC shifts during the same event will award 40 Service Points instead of Bonus BP (or 30 SP if the shift was not signed up for in advance during pre-registration).
 
Bonus BP
All players may earn Bonus Build Points (BP) for their character, up to a cap of their total character level. Bonus BP can be earned in the following ways:

      * Full-event NPCing grants 2 Bonus BP, plus Service Points, plus additional exclusive rewards (see tab “Full-Event NPC Incentives”)

      * A player’s first voluntary 4-hour NPC shift at each event grants 1 Bonus BP. (Additional shifts at the same event earn Service Points.)

      * Earned Service Points can be spent on Bonus BP (see “How to Spend Service Points”).

      * Optionally, a monetary donation of $40, limited to once per event attended. Note: This is not meant to be cost-effective - our team values volunteer time more highly than material donations, and our intent is for any player to be able to max out their Bonus BP without spending any extra money!
Service Points
Service Points (SP) are awarded to each player for a variety of volunteer tasks, and redeemed for in-game perks. Note: Service Points cannot be earned through monetary donations.
How to Earn Service Points
Full-Event NPC:
On top of other rewards, full-event NPCs receive 150 Service Points per event, or 200 for season finale events. For more details, see tab “Full-Event NPC Incentives”.


Apply for a Player Helper Role:
Player Helper roles are dedicated, recurring support roles for players that require specific skills or expertise. For descriptions of each Player Helper role and their awarded Service Points per event, see the “Wellspring Staff and Player Helper Roles” tab.


Extra Service Shifts:
During events, players may volunteer their time on an hourly basis for Service Points. This can either be signed up for in advance during pre-registration (which gives extra SP), or on site during the game. These shifts will not have unlimited availability, and in general, combat NPCs will be in highest demand, and others will have a small amount of hours needed per event.


Note: All players are required to sign up for a minimum of one “Cleanup Duty” of their choice during each event - this is most commonly post-game cleanup of a specified area, but players who need to leave early may sign up for pre-game or mid-game tasks as their Cleanup Duty instead. The below SP rates refer to any volunteering from players beyond their required Cleanup Duty.


         * NPCing: 40 SP per extra shift pre-reg, 30 SP per shift on-site (Note, this is only for extra shifts beyond an initial shift, which always grants 1 Bonus BP).

         * Pre-Game Setup: 10 SP per hour pre-reg, 5 SP per hour on-site. This may involve unloading the trailer, cleaning and prepping game areas, etc.

         * Mid-Game Chores: 10 SP per hour pre-reg, 5 SP per hour on-site. This may involve tidying, encounter set up, meal clean-up (cleaning surfaces, handling leftovers, cleaning dishes), etc.

         * Monster Camp Support: 10 SP per hour pre-reg, 5 SP per hour on-site. This may involve applying make-up, costuming/prepping NPCs, organizing/cleaning monster camp, etc.

         * Wherever Needed: Variable SP per hour pre-reg, Variable SP per hour on-site. Staff places this role into whatever shift is required most at staff's discretion - the award rate is set to whatever the highest rewards are for minimum/maximum.


Support the Game Between Events:
Many production tasks also need to be done before and after each event, and players can choose to contribute their time for Service Points as well.

            * Work Event Attendee: 50 SP per event. Work events are staff-organized opportunities for players to attend and volunteer. Each work event will have details on what is expected from attendees. Examples include a site cleanup day to help maintain the game site, or a shift advertising the game at a local convention.

            * Production Assistance: 25 SP per event, additional SP for time spent. A Player Helper role, Production Assistants will help with between-game production tasks. These tasks will be tailored to the skills of the individual production assistant. This could involve helping make props, build or repair weapons, make packets, designing music, etc! Applicants to the Production Assistant role should tell the staff team what production-related skills they have when applying. In all cases, production tasks that contain game spoilers will not be assigned to Production Assistants. Material costs will be covered by Wellspring, and time spent will award additional SP. 

            * Community Event Organizer: 50 SP per event, with staff approval. Players may organize smaller social events or fighter practices for Wellspring players between scheduled events, with staff approval.

            * Community Event Attendee: 5 SP per approved event. Examples of community events can be seen above under “Community Event Organizer.”
How to Spend Service Points
The following are the available rewards and their Service Point costs. Any reward may be purchased on another player’s behalf, with their permission.


Retrain Power: 20 SP
Remove one Class Power from your character sheet and replace it with another Class Power of equal or lower Tier.


Edit Spellbook: 20 SP
Replace a Known Spell in your spellbook for another of equal or lower Tier.


Alter Recent Class: 30 SP
Replace your character’s most recent class level with another. You must meet all the requirements for the new class. This will also apply to Advanced Class levels when they are released.


Refund BP Purchase: 10 SP per BP
Remove a purchased Skill, Perk, or Domain Power and refund the BP cost. Note that all BP refunds must be approved by Staff, and some may have additional requirements to refund (such as Inheritance).


Purchase Missed XP: 200 SP per event
If a player did not attend a Wellspring event, they may purchase the amount of experience points their character would have received by attending.


Purchase Bonus BP: 100 SP
A character may only have a total amount of Bonus BP equal to their character level.


Legacy Magic Item: 800 SP
This may only be purchased once ever. A Legacy Item is a potent magic item that is custom tailored to your character (and only attunable by your character), increases in power at every season finale, and will be rebuilt for your character if you choose to play a new character (or your original character dies or is retired).


Draw from the Copper Bag: 20 SP
Draw from the Silver Bag: 45 SP
Draw from the Gold Bag: 90 SP
A Grab Bag Draw is a semi-randomized reward. The Copper Bag contains mostly common Resources, with a small chance at uncommon. The Silver Bag contains mostly uncommon Resources, with a small chance at rare. The Gold Bag contains mostly rare Resources, with a small chance at a free Build Point or even a Legacy Magic Item! Full contents of each Grab Bag and their draw rates can be found in the SP Tracker: 


Custom Recipe: 100 SP
The pinnacle of every craftsman’s career is an invention or masterwork that alters the entire landscape of their craft. These revelations often bear the name of their inventor as they broaden the understanding of their craft, and gain notoriety for their work. Players selecting this option have the opportunity to collaborate with Staff to create a new recipe for one craft, and once approved, have their name or appellation attached to the crafting recipe’s name.


Master Casting: 30 SP per incant line
The character may write their own spell incantation. This incantation must be approved by Staff before it is used, and will be logged and permanent for that character.


A Special Invitation: 200 SP, maximum once per player per event 
Note: These will be accepted at Staff discretion when time permits - there may not be space available at particular events. Redemption must be approved in advance.
Step right up! Step right up! The Traveling Star needs YOU - that's right, YOU for a brand new pilot episode! The purchaser of this Special Invitation will be brought on as the celebrity contestant, complete with their own post game interview! (Other participants will be included. The Special Invitation binds the invitee to settle all disputes as to safety, game rules, or media interpretation in arbitration).
Full-Event NPC Incentives
Wellspring Full-Event NPC Incentives
Wellspring aims to be a game that can field large, complex encounters without requiring mandatory NPC shifts. To do so, we need the help of people willing (and excited) to play as NPCs for entire events (full-event NPCing). To help this, we have designed a series of benefits full-event NPCs receive as a thank-you for helping the game be the best it can be. In all cases we will endeavor to make NPCing with us as fun as possible, working with you to find roles you will enjoy. We think we have an exciting and compelling story to help tell - and you will be an integral member in having that come to life!


Games are free, and meals (or a meal plan) will be provided:
Players are welcome to full-event NPC without paying any game fees. Meals will be provided: NPCs will either be included in staff meals/food prep, or they will be given a free ticket to the meal plan (details of any potential meal plan are TBD). Coming to afters? Chance will personally buy you dinner.


Increased Character Advancement:
Players who play full-event NPCs will receive two additional benefits to direct character advancement: the first of which is they gain 200% experience, while under the campaign maximum. The second of which is that they will receive 2 Bonus BP towards their maximum (cap is the character’s total level - see Bonus BP).


Additional Above-Cap BP:
The first time in a season you are a full-event NPC you will receive 2 free BP for your character on top of all other advancements - this is not Bonus BP and can bring you above your maximum. Additionally, full-event NPCing season finale events will earn you 1 additional free BP that also does not count towards your maximum bonus.


Service Points:
Full-event NPCing will earn you 150 Service Points (200 for season finales). Service Points can be exchanged for a variety of perks for your character - including a Legacy Magic Item: this is a potent magic item that is custom tailored to your character, levels up at every season finale, and will be rebuilt for your character if you choose to play a new character (or your original character dies or is retired).


Longer-Term Recurring Roles:
If you agree to be a full-event NPC for us for a longer time period (3-4 games) and desire more tailored NPC opportunities, we will slot you into our pool of recurring NPC roles. We will work with you to figure out what you most enjoy playing, and the role can be anything from a large recurring villain or mini-boss to a small emotion-focused part - all based on your desires/needs.


Spoiler Free-ish NPC Experience:
At the center of our story is a series of large mysteries. When you are NPCing, we will be careful not to reveal any secret lore/story beats to you (or any of the other NPCs). We want to preserve the mystery of the game for you, regardless of if you decide to help us by NPCing!


Our Eternal Love and Gratitude:
Seriously! Any game needs a solid core of dedicated full-event NPCs. You will have the eternal love and respect from the entire staff team (who are all full-event NPCs) by helping us make this story a reality.


Wellspring Staff and Player Helper Roles
Wellspring Staff and Player Helper Roles
